posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
Nick Ryan:

The Bordelais, up-and-down the ranks, are making what people want to buy. If you like traditional Bordeaux, go out there and support those who still make it with your money. If all the numerous modernist-skeptics on this thread are in fact doing so, kudos, but apparently the vast majority of the market doesn’t share your preferences; to that I can only “c’est la vie”. If you aren’t doing so, you’re the cause of the problem far more than Parker is.

Nick, this may come as a shock to you, but Bordeaux, as the most popular wine in the world (at least it was; I am not sure if that has changed), is what I will call a lowest-common-denominator product…blended to a formula, easy to understand, produced in huge quantities, embraceable by those with undeveloped palates and often little real interest in the larger world of fine wines. Coincidence that, until recently, British food was among the worst on earth and Great Britain was Bordeaux’s number-one market for most of its history? No. A joint of beast and claret. A Flannery’s 45-day aged rib cap and a 2009 Cos in Monkton. Same thing. The most popular style of ANYTHING is rarely, if ever, the best. Chips Ahoy or grandma’s Toll House cookies right out of the oven? Toyota Camry or Maserati Quattroporto? Men’s Wearhouse or Gucci? Budweiser or outstanding craft beer? I could give you, say, 100,000 easy examples to the same effect. (Note that the better or best is not even necessarily or always about the most expensive; granny’s cookies cost less to make than Chips Ahoy do to buy.) At the absurd prices being charged for it, high-end Bordeaux needs to be a Maserati, not a Camry, but in fact, just the opposite has happened. What has happened is that a blue-collar Camry salesman in rural Maryland, one with no taste for or understanding of several of the world’s most finely crafted automobiles, has sold a bill of goods to a large number of people who started the day knowing nothing about fine wine, just like their guru. It has not just hurt Maserati sales. It has wrecked the Maserati and left it rusting in a ditch on the side of the road. That being the case, forgive those of us who go on the offensive against inexperienced drinkers who have been brainwashed into accepting lowest-common-denominator wines rather than exploring and understanding great ones, at all price points. At $20 a bottle, maybe I would not give a damn; at $500-3,000 a bottle en primeur, I tend to care a lot more. Of course, a high percentage of those wines remain the most traditionally made, and command a premium for it…

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: Someone once asked Julia Child what she thought of McDonalds hamburgers. Instead of an elitist response, she said something along the line of that she thought that it was pretty good for what it was, and could be depended upon. While I can and do appreciate the passion of an ideologue and zealot, someone who has both the taste and resources to enjoy the very best, such as first growth Bordeaux, Giacosa, and Conterno’s Monfortino, also has the luxury of theorizing what should be for the masses. People who are quite serious about wine don’t necessarily have the inclination to patiently wait decades for their wines to become drinkable, if not enjoyable. A legitimate case can be made that the improvements made in winemaking that are held in such disdain by tradionalist purists have done great things for the enhancement of the enjoyment of wine, yes even by those who are serious enthusiasts, if not curmudgeons lamenting for the days of the past.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
bob poirier:

WOW, you people are arrogant a holes to think that the majority of consumers continue to prefer modern style wines because of Parker and not their own preference, and you few, who can think for yourselves, stand on your soapbox and try to save of us from ourselves. I agree with Nick, the market will dictate, not Parker. The majority of buyers in the above $30 range know what they like and know what they are getting.

What part of “Parker created the modernist market and, through his use of the absurd 100-point system that isn’t, compelled many winemakers to please his palate or perish” do you not understand, Bob? They continue to prefer the modern style because it is all they know and they need the security blanket of Parker points. It is the security of the pack. The self-righteousness of being in the majority, as if that always, or even often, ends up being the smart position. And, God bless it and all, the U.S. of A. does not have a rosy “majority” history. Remember the “silent majority” that kept the Vietnam War quagmire going? I don’t mean to dredge that up and get political; I use it only to point out the shortcomings of alleged “majority rule”.

Another point for all camps: do we know for a fact that the modern style of Bordeaux is in fact dominant, and if so, on what economic basis? For instance (and I do not say that this is true), let’s assume that the first growths may have been impacted by some combination of Parkerization and climate change, but remain essentially traditional wines. Consider the dollar or Euro volume of total Bordeaux sales that they represent, and what percentage that is of total Bordeaux sales. Certainly, I have to believe that, at the lower end, a clear majority of the wines are modernized, in order to find a market at all. Fruity, oaky, nondescript wines exist at the lower end of virtually every wine-producing region on earth. (Residual sugar always helps, too.) Then we have the vast middle ground, where many historically mediocre properties with no claim to terroir have been anointed by Parker for making wines that suit him and other traditional properties keep on doing what they do in spite of Parker’s influence. I am not trying to dispute the impact of Parkerization. Just wondering if it is as bad as its opponents fear…

posts.name: David S. Corrick
posts.cooked: I must say that I have found this discussion thread by far the most informative and entertaining on Bezerker thus far! I have purchased a very modest amount of Bordeaux throughout the 2000’s and there is no denying that the influence of Parker and his acolytes have resulted in significant stylistic changes in the product. Previously my comfort level and expectation for certain estates was unwavering, however the developments of the last decade or two have left me increasing skeptical of BB’s and driven me to explore other regions/styles/varietals more vigorously. I’m thinking I’m not alone in this observation or shift in preferences and while I suppose there will always be a demand for “Parkeriszed” style of BB’s; I’m hopeful that as with all things this trend is cyclical and some producers will trend back to a more traditional style in the future. In the meantime…the rest of the world’s wine awaits.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
Michael S. Monie:

Someone once asked Julia Child what she thought of McDonalds hamburgers. Instead of an elitist response, she said something along the line of that she thought that it was pretty good for what it was, and could be depended upon. While I can and do appreciate the passion of an ideologue and zealot, someone who has both the taste and resources to enjoy the very best, such as first growth Bordeaux, Giacosa, and Conterno’s Monfortino, also has the luxury of theorizing what should be for the masses. People who are quite serious about wine don’t necessarily have the inclination to patiently wait decades for their wines to become drinkable, if not enjoyable. A legitimate case can be made that the improvements made in winemaking that are held in such disdain by tradionalist purists have done great things for the enhancement of the enjoyment of wine, yes even by those who are serious enthusiasts, if not curmudgeons lamenting for the days of the past.

But Michael, early-drinking wines will never be at risk. They never were before Parker. Nebbiolo instead of Barolo or Barbaresco. Rosso di Montalcino instead of Brunello. Lesser-growth Bordeaux instead of the first growths. Village Burgundy instead of cru. I could go on. The argument is that something that was not broken is being “fixed” for no good reason. And let’s be very clear about one thing: old farts like me can afford the very best not always because we are all rolling in the deep, but because we figured out what the best was by tasting broadly and put it away for less money, even adjusted for inflation, than some are paying for fruity, woody plonk today. Our patience has been rewarded. I absolutely hear you on the impatience refrain, and do not deny your right to it, but unfortunately, most of the world’s greatest wines, regardless of cost, do not lend themselves to early drinking except in selected vintages. Impatience has an obvious advantage, and an enormous cost in terms of potential long-term loss of quality and complexity. One of the really absurd things about modernist thinking is that people want to have their cake and eat it, too: they want to be able to drink their wines on release, but at the same time, are obsessed with the same wines lasting for 30-50 years and improving. (And some actually believe Parker’s proselytizing, speculative BS about ageworthiness.) Not enough time has passed to know if that is possible, but the evidence thus far suggests probably not. All modern style wines are not clones of the 1947 Cheval Blanc, itself a total anomaly…

Someone must have been making wines in a style Parker liked before he started tasting, so Parker didn’t create it, he may have helped introduce it to people who didn’t appreciate thin acidic dirty wines they had been exposed to. It’s no different than what people like Gilman and Fiering do with championing the AFWE wines. There is plenty of wine out there made in all styles, stop complaining and drink what you like including Yellowtail. I’m not even going to touch the majority rule, it’s fermented grape juice lets not forget.

Rudi Sprechen Sie bitte Langsam

In Parker’s case, the 1982 Bordeaux vintage came first. It did not suit the Brits, but it did suit an ex-lawyer in Monkton, MD who knew next to nothing about wine at the time. With justification, he appreciated the ripe style of the 1982 vintage. He then went on to make the fatal mistake of thinking that all of the world’s wines should taste like 1982 Bordeaux. Suggesting that winemaking should be cleaner does not change the fundamental style; it merely eliminates flaws, but even there, sometimes also a bit of what makes the wine fascinating as well. (Parker likes plenty of funky, bretty Chateauneufs, by the way, so Mr. Clean he ain’t, exactly.) He really cannot account well for that one-trick-pony philosophy on the one hand, and all those 13% alcohol, balanced fruit/acid/tannins/oak Bordeaux that preceded the 1982 vintage that are dramatically better than the 1982s and carry his magic 100 points on the other. Ditto old Napa Cabs. He does not even try. He just rants on self-righteously. He rails against Burgundy with inadequate tasting experience to even be offering opinions.

posts.name: David S. Corrick
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:
Michael S. Monie:

Someone once asked Julia Child what she thought of McDonalds hamburgers. Instead of an elitist response, she said something along the line of that she thought that it was pretty good for what it was, and could be depended upon. While I can and do appreciate the passion of an ideologue and zealot, someone who has both the taste and resources to enjoy the very best, such as first growth Bordeaux, Giacosa, and Conterno’s Monfortino, also has the luxury of theorizing what should be for the masses. People who are quite serious about wine don’t necessarily have the inclination to patiently wait decades for their wines to become drinkable, if not enjoyable. A legitimate case can be made that the improvements made in winemaking that are held in such disdain by tradionalist purists have done great things for the enhancement of the enjoyment of wine, yes even by those who are serious enthusiasts, if not curmudgeons lamenting for the days of the past.

But Michael, early-drinking wines will never be at risk. They never were before Parker. Nebbiolo instead of Barolo or Barbaresco. Rosso di Montalcino instead of Brunello. Lesser-growth Bordeaux instead of the first growths. Village Burgundy instead of cru. I could go on. The argument is that something that was not broken is being “fixed” for no good reason. And let’s be very clear about one thing: old farts like me can afford the very best not always because we are all rolling in the deep, but because we figured out what the best was by tasting broadly and put it away for less money, even adjusted for inflation, than some are paying for fruity, woody plonk today. Our patience has been rewarded. I absolutely hear you on the impatience refrain, and do not deny your right to it, but unfortunately, most of the world’s greatest wines, regardless of cost, do not lend themselves to early drinking except in selected vintages. Impatience has an obvious advantage, and an enormous cost in terms of potential long-term loss of quality and complexity. One of the really absurd things about modernist thinking is that people want to have their cake and eat it, too: they want to be able to drink their wines on release, but at the same time, are obsessed with the same wines lasting for 30-50 years and improving. (And some actually believe Parker’s proselytizing, speculative BS about ageworthiness.) Not enough time has passed to know if that is possible, but the evidence thus far suggests probably not. All modern style wines are not clones of the 1947 Cheval Blanc, itself a total anomaly…

Excellent points Bill and therein is the conundrum. The stylistic changes over the last decade have created a clientele that have an expectation to drink 100 pt BB’s that will also improve with age and last for decades. Certainly possible but completely unrealistic.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
Should not Parker be vindicated for his assessment of the 82 vintage? Having recently dispatched the remainder of his very large collection at the charity dinner he hosted for the benefit of the Navy Seals, the majority of the wines apparently showed very well at 30 years of age. Not all Bordeaux ever has or ever will last 50+ years.

The issue of the level of tannin extraction in modern styled Bordeaux can be determined with analysis. Although the wines are significantly more approachable than the vintages when mouth puckering tannins were the norm, the bones for graceful maturation and significant longevity may very well be present. It may just be a case of having your cake and eating it too.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
bob poirier:

Sorry Rudi experience does not exclude one from being an a hole. I find it laughable every time someone blames Parker for the state of the wine industry, refusing to believe that a majority of drinkers actually enjoy the modern style of wine and are not just lemmings. just because we enjoy different styles of wine doesn’t make one palate more sophisticated, just different.

Do you disagree that people who are willing to spend more on wine are more likely to be more knowledgeable and have more experience with wine?

I believe most wine consumers don’t cellar. It is easier to enjoy young modern style Bordeaux, opulent fruit expression, a hint or obvious vanillin oak, round and silky palate and controlled tannins. The issue is some Chateaus push this to the extreme and if you add a factor of poor vintage and terroir, the wine can become pruneny, oak dominating, hollow in the middle and noticeable dry tannins. In general, well made modern Bordeaux is quite nice to drink young.

For me, I prefer the modern Bordeaux to the 60s and the 70s in general. The 75s are finally coming along and this only helps a very few hardcore Bordeaux lovers.

I know people make fun but I think Yellow Tail, Kendal Jackson VR Chardonnay, DP and etc do great job producing great products. In case of DP, something quite special.

Michael, absolutely not. This is harsh, but I am convinced that it was an example of the proverbial blind pig finding the acorn now and again. The 1982s are still showing well, occasionally brilliantly, but so are well-kept 1945 first-growths, as well as many 1947s, 1959s and 1961s (I have never had an 1982 that touches the best that I have had from those vintages, by the way), not to mention a couple of large fistfuls of great old Bordeaux in the 75-100-year-old range. (I would not use the Navy Seals event as a benchmark, but do not want to create a distraction by discussing my misgivings about that event.) I am not arguing against potential longevity of modern style Bordeaux as much as questioning the likelihood of fascinating secondary and tertiary characteristics developing. I have not found that to happen in modernist Nebbiolo or the few modernist Burgundies that I have tasted, but again, we are not able to test much beyond 30 years in any region at this point…

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: One other stray data point that seems to be overlooked in this thread: due to climate change, along with some changes in winemaking techniques that do not rise to modernist winemaking, there are more than a few traditional wines that drink well young in many vintages, and, obviously, for my taste, drink better than modernist wines from the same vintages and regions. Of course, that is not going to be a given as it is with wines that are vinified for early drinking…

Bill,
A bunch of Barolo heads and I tasted the 90 Barolos blind. The objective was whether it was possible to identify modernist vs traditionalist. No one was able to do so. Just an observation.

Oak will dissipate in some well-made modernist Barolos, so that is not surprising to me. However, as you move back in time with modernist Baroli, I think that you will find a lot of them fading, while you will find many traditional Baroli at each vintage crossroads just entering the window of drinkability. Of course, other factors, vineyard quality and many other things, go into the equation.

We need you to do the same thing for a batch of Bordeaux when they have enough age on them to make you comfortable with the test. I think that 20 years is probably enough for the Barolo test. I am less certain about the right age for a comparable Bordeaux test.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Kevin Shin:

IMO, Bob did an excellent job. IMO, Bob did a great job at least until the 00. If one bought the wines based on his recommendation, they would have been happy, like the 86 Rauzan Segla, 86 Talbot, 75 Giscours and etc.

I liked many of Parker’s recommendations back in the '80s. The problem IMO was his general philosophy (which I believe he picked up from Peynaud?) that wines age on their fruit. He has stated that since acid stays constant and fruit declines the most ageworthy wines are those where there’s a lot of fruit and low acid.

I have not found that to be true in my experience.

And of course there’s the whole point escalation thing.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked: IMO, Michel Roland and Stephane Derenoncourt make very different style of wine. Perhaps the 09s and the 10s in 2030 will give a very good indication.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
bob poirier:

…Do you disagree that people who are willing to spend more on wine are more likely to be more knowledgeable and have more experience with wine?

Money is essential, no question, but the vast majority of wealthy people is as experienced or inexperienced as all the others, and most of them have no clue - as all the others. The only difference is, that they have more money and can afford more expensive wines. But even this does not necessarily have to mean that those wines are better than cheaper wines…

It is a long, long way to become an experienced Bordeaux lover or even a connoisseur. You have to read dozens and dozens of specialist books over the years and decades, and you have to taste thousands and thousands of wines from all Bordeaux appellations, from top to the bottom. This is extremely time consuming.

More than 8000 vignerons make more than 40000 different wines in Bordeaux, and just about 3-5 % are wines in the above $30 range. In the $15-30 range, certainly more than 10 % of the entire output in Bordeaux, you can have more excellent and outstanding wines than in the $30-60 range. But without knowledge and experience you will not have a chance to find them, and that has nothing to do with money.

In the above $30 range you pay for the name, not first of all for the quality, and the fact, that a millionaire can afford Lafite, Haut-Brion or Le Pin does not make him a knowledgeable person. Not in the slightest!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Ed Murray:
Robert Alfert:
Nick Ryan:

The Bordelais, up-and-down the ranks, are making what people want to buy. If you like traditional Bordeaux, go out there and support those who still make it with your money. If all the numerous modernist-skeptics on this thread are in fact doing so, kudos, but apparently the vast majority of the market doesn’t share your preferences; to that I can only “c’est la vie”. If you aren’t doing so, you’re the cause of the problem far more than Parker is.

My sister texted me yesterday: “Which critic’s rating do you follow?” I asked why, she said “because my local wine store has shelf talkers with Parker, Spectator and Enthusiast”. I told her they were using the highest score to push the wine. She said, “does it matter, a 90 is a 90, right?” Then she bought the Lanessan cause it got a 90. That is the market Parker and his ilk created. My sister has no clue whether Lanessan is traditional or modern, good or crappy. She doesn’t even know if it tastes like wood and vodka, or red fruit and earth, cause the shelf talker only touted a number. She only knows it got a 90.

Yet… she bought the Lanessan. Last time I checked Lannesan is very far from “modern.”

True, it was a great choice! But, sadly, a choice based on a score not on the essence of this estate. She knows nothing about Lanessan, Medoc, modern or traditional. She only knows it’s a Bordeaux, cost $20 and got a 90. And gracefully, she is bringing it to Thanksgiving dinner, so great choice for me!

posts.name: Nick Ryan
posts.cooked:
Jim Brennan:

The issue, apparently lost on many, is that a good portion of the so-called “market” follows the breadcrumb trail of points left in the wake of Parker, WS, etc., often without much knowledge or context regarding non-modernist Bordeaux wines, beyond Parker’s damning them with faint praise.

The market has spoken? Not so much IMO. The more accurate statement might be that the market has done as it was told!

But you’re missing the point. People who are buying modern Bordeaux are not going to stop because you tell them to. They’re not going to stop if Parker retires. They will continue to buy because they like the wines. As the example of Bob’s Aussie Shiraz misadventure has proven, people will not continue to buy wines they don’t like because Bob orders them too. Bordeaux traditionalists have to accept the fact they cannot re-educate the modernists, and instead will have to take solace in simple economics – start buying more traditional Bordeaux, and therefore encourage more Chateaux to cater to your needs.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: Nick, I cannot buy your logic. The fact is that most wine drinkers have life cycles. They start drinking the cheap, easy-to-understand stuff, and then graduate to more expensive, more complicated stuff. Back in the day, I chased some Parker points and drank some oaky Cali Chardonnay and Markham Merlot and a bunch of plonk that seemed great to me at the time. I tasted. I learned. I outgrew those wines and Parker. Nobody is looking to have anybody stop drinking modernist wines because we say so. If anything, are trying to keep traditional wines from disappearing, not just by buying them, but also by educating the next generation about the sins of the Parker having been visited upon the lemmings. Meanwhile, many drinking modern wines right now will outgrow it. And many will change their spots when there are no Parker points to chase. You are giving some in that camp too much credit by assuming that they have a taste for certain wines independent of points…

posts.name: Bruce G
posts.cooked:
Nick Ryan:

instead will have to take solace in simple economics – start buying more traditional Bordeaux, and therefore encourage more Chateaux to cater to your needs.

Is that really how it works, Nick?
Many of the Bordeaux makers that have gone “modern” were selling through their wines easily enough even back when they were making traditional wines (exceptions noted for genres like the St. Em garagistes, a class of wines that didn’t really exist until late, and therefore never had a traditional incarnation).
The economic motivation to change is, in many cases, not so much “to sell wine”, but “to sell wine at a much higher price”. The Bordelaise have always been extremely concerned with the price of their wine v. those of their neighbors; look at how much the landscape is still impacted by the asking price circa 1855.
Points allow for more aggressive pricing. I do not know of any alternative current model that would allow a Bordeaux chateau to jack up its prices so greatly. I guess if a traditionalist chateau came out and said “unless you traditionalist customers start paying us 150% more per bottle we’re going to go the modern route” that might serve, theoretically. But for all sorts of reasons no winery is going to do this.

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
I’m not nearly old enough to have tasted Bordeaux in the 80s or earlier so it would be difficult for me to compare older Bordeaux that I have tasted more recently with more modern expressions of Bordeaux. Reading through this thread makes me wonder if there’s an element of nostalgia at play but at the same time I have to at least entertain the idea that it’s a fully legitimate concern. We have yet to taste many of these modern examples with any significant bottle age and it may prove to be that the substance and character of the wine remains under the ripe qualities it has now. That’s not necessarily an argument in favor of them, but merely an observation that we deal with the unknown.

Regardless I have to think that if that which is traditional is “good” for it’s own sake (in a philosophical sense) and not merely perception of that which is comfortable, then at some point it will be re-discovered again despite the real or perceived machinations of wine critics. Winemaking despite having economic realities is a matter of passion and art. Even now we see young winemakers in California pursuing “terroir” and more balanced wines. Whether this is idle talk or not I can’t say but I genuinely believe that the pendulum may swing back.

Tastes can be cyclical in all arenas. Design, architecture and even wine-making. No doubt many architects in their time decried the contemporary forms in recent history but it would hardly seem fair for them to criticize the passer-by who admires the clean lines and simple forms. In the same way there is likely some good in the modern wines we see today. Like in all things though it’s a matter of balance. So let the pendulum swing and we will see where we are in a few more years.

Nick, I cannot buy your logic. The fact is that most wine drinkers have life cycles. They start drinking the cheap, easy-to-understand stuff, and then graduate to more expensive, more complicated stuff. Back in the day, I chased some Parker points and drank some oaky Cali Chardonnay and Markham Merlot and a bunch of plonk that seemed great to me at the time. I tasted. I learned. I outgrew those wines and Parker. Nobody is looking to have anybody stop drinking modernist wines because we say so. If anything, are trying to keep traditional wines from disappearing, not just by buying them, but also by educating the next generation about the sins of the Parker having been visited upon the lemmings. Meanwhile, many drinking modern wines right now will outgrow it. And many will change their spots when there are no Parker points to chase. You are giving some in that camp too much credit by assuming that they have a taste for certain wines independent of points…

Bill, you nailed it. Development of an own, independent taste is a privilege. A privilege you have to earn over time. It is also a permanent emancipation process. One who is not willing to learn remains a lemming. This hold true not only for inexperienced wine lovers, but also for connoisseurs and critics.

The amount of pontification and speculation about other drinkers here is just staggering. I feel like Moses is about to deliver the stone Bordeaux tablets at any moment. Salvation is at hand. Soon we heathens will be saved from our love of Pavie and possession by Parker.

Man do some of you take this seriously. This could go for 100 more pages an we’d all be right where we started, with our own tastes.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:

Not to deny at all that Parker worked for you, at least for a time, but I believe also that you are a child of the Parker generation that happens to have gone broader over the years.

To be fair, an awful lot of people here were also Parker Children and came to their current taste in Bordeaux through his notes. I expect a lot of people today are doing the same, and will figure out which styles they like. I figured out that a 2nd-ish growth wine that he rated 92 or 93 was likely to be right in my sweet spot. It might be tougher today since there is more divergence in style and most critics aren’t doing a very good job of pointing it out (I’m still wondering what I’ll think when I open a 98 or 00 Pape Clement… at the time it just sounded like if you liked it before, just wait until you taste the new ones!).

Man do some of you take this seriously. This could go for 100 more pages an we’d all be right where we started, with our own tastes.

posts.cooked:

Not to deny at all that Parker worked for you, at least for a time, but I believe also that you are a child of the Parker generation that happens to have gone broader over the years.

To be fair, an awful lot of people here were also Parker Children and came to their current taste in Bordeaux through his notes.

Bingo, Craig. And many now suffer arrested development and a myopic view of wine as a consequence. They dig in their heels like Jeff Leve, John Gonzales and others and vigorously defend the right to be myopic, rather than making the effort to broaden their horizons. Preferences are one thing; sticking one’s head in the sand quite another. Posts like John’s perfectly frame the problem. He contributes nothing to the discussion, offers no defenses of the modernist style, just spouts off in an effort to stifle the dialogue of intelligent and articulate people whose opinions appear to make him insecure, angry and whatever else. Hey, John…you are wasting your breath. We do take this seriously, and will continue to. If you have any actual thoughts of the subject of traditional vs. modern Bordeaux, express them. Otherwise, please do not waste the time of those who do with empty carping…

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
TBroussard:

I’m not nearly old enough to have tasted Bordeaux in the 80s or earlier so it would be difficult for me to compare older Bordeaux that I have tasted more recently with more modern expressions of Bordeaux. Reading through this thread makes me wonder if there’s an element of nostalgia at play but at the same time I have to at least entertain the idea that it’s a fully legitimate concern. We have yet to taste many of these modern examples with any significant bottle age and it may prove to be that the substance and character of the wine remains under the ripe qualities it has now. That’s not necessarily an argument in favor of them, but merely an observation that we deal with the unknown.

Regardless I have to think that if that which is traditional is “good” for it’s own sake (in a philosophical sense) and not merely perception of that which is comfortable, then at some point it will be re-discovered again despite the real or perceived machinations of wine critics. Winemaking despite having economic realities is a matter of passion and art. Even now we see young winemakers in California pursuing “terroir” and more balanced wines. Whether this is idle talk or not I can’t say but I genuinely believe that the pendulum may swing back.

Tastes can be cyclical in all arenas. Design, architecture and even wine-making. No doubt many architects in their time decried the contemporary forms in recent history but it would hardly seem fair for them to criticize the passer-by who admires the clean lines and simple forms. In the same way there is likely some good in the modern wines we see today. Like in all things though it’s a matter of balance. So let the pendulum swing and we will see where we are in a few more years.

Thanks for your thoughtful post! The postulation is that winemakers and their consultants find their way back to the essential values elegance and balance. This is a universal claim, but not a right, of course. From this perspective a refocusing on these values is inevitable. But the banal question remains: When will they do it? In a hundred years?

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
No, Bob, deciding to further one’s wine education and broaden one’s tasting experience is the personal choice and personal preference. Once one decides to do that, the “educated taste” is a fact and the logical consequence of the educational process. What any given person thinks of any given wine is indeed subjective, but, over time, wine drinkers build concensus on which of the world’s wines are the best, best for the money, or whatever other yardstick may be used from time to time. And, like it or not, that process results in something not far from objectivity. Not one person in a million could taste a pristine bottle of 1990 La Tache or 1989 Haut-Brion blind and not be blown away by the experience. I submit to you that this would be true even for those who do not like Burgundy or Bordeaux. (I use obvious, easy examples; there are many less rare and less expensive wines about which I could make the same point.)

The problem arises when the concensus of experienced wine drinkers butts heads with Parker and the rest of the wine-reviewing community. Parker’s opinion is one; the experienced are many, and Parker was sucking down Coca-Cola when he decided that wine was pretty good stuff. Parker has become arrogant, close-minded and self-righteous in his views; the experienced often vigorously debate one another, rarely afraid to disagree, using the dialectic to learn even more. Parker and his ilk pretend to be consumer’s advocates, but in fact, end up doing little more than moving product, even getting medals from governments of wine-producing countries in appreciation of all the product that got moved; the experienced just drink the stuff. Parker and his ilk taste 10,000 wines a year, spending seconds or minutes with most of them, and then presume to render a trite, inarticulate Cliff’s Notes judgment on the wine, complete with points from a scale that is demonstrable bullshit, both in concept and application; the experienced often spend hours or days with a single bottle, years or decades tasting a single wine repeatedly, for their own education and enjoyment. Over time, Parker’s palate has become immune to oak and tannins, and he appears to be incapable of appreciating anything but fruit bombs that have been made to order for him. His palate, never much to begin with, is shot. He needs to retire with what little grace and dignity he can muster.

That taste in wine is subjective answers nothing, and is not the beginning nor the end of the story. Too many people with more disposable income than taste or judgment pick up the Wine Advocate or Wine Spectator and rush out to buy wines that they have never tasted because Parker or Suckling dealt the wines scores of 96-100. (Admittedly, one has to start somewhere, so I cannot fault a person for succumbing to the reviewers’ siren songs in the absence of other available information.) After a while, they have cellars and storage lockers full of the same wine. It is what they prefer because it is all that they know. And the reviewers constantly churn out the big scores for the same wines, with only the vintages changing the numbers slightly. It is wine’s answer to a hamster wheel, not the settling on a preferred style after years of experiencing thousands of different wines. Most of the people who are violently anti-Burgundy know nothing about Burgundy. They do not drink it because Parker does not drink or understand it, and he has told the sheep (giving lemmings a rest for a moment) that Burgundy is a minefield of shrill, green, acidic wines. (Of course , all of the old Parker 96-100s on Burgundies would seem to make him a short-sighted BSer on that count. He was never able to figure out that Burgundy is an extremely ageworthy wine, and that the best rarely reveal their extraordinary qualities without many years in the bottle.) Maybe the occasional sheep will sekk out a few bottles of Burgundy, do no homework, drink it too young while dumb, have a “Bob is right” a-ha moment and go back to drinking Cabernet, Syrah and Grenache-based fruit bombs. That, Bob, is the reality that we confront. The experienced rarely, if ever, lock themselves into a single wine or style. We have many people on this board that drink nothing but high-octane California wines, or those and high-octane wines from Bordeaux and the rest of the world. That does make their palates and opinions count for less. They are entitled to like what they like, but must do so in the midst of those who know not only such wines, but thousands of others as well. It is a real pity that those who know less are so often resentful of those who know more, and can do nothing but attempt to discredit superior knowledge. Make no mistake: this is rarely, if ever, about superior noses and palates…it is about superior knowledge and experience. “Completely subjective” is a cop-out, not a viable argument.

Sounds like a lot of people in this topic. So Parker is not an experienced,knowledgeable taster, but you and Rudi are? This post is so arrogant it is mind boggling. Always the Parker followers are sheep and unknowledgeable, how could they be otherwise since they don’t share your preference? How could anyone who has tasted 10’s of thousands of wines for 3 or 4 decades not agree with you, assuming they become knowledgeable and experienced.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked: Bob, I give up with you.

posts.name: bob poirier
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:

Bob, I give up with you.

Don’t give up yet!There is still hope for me.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: No, bob, Parker is an experienced taster, but one who has constantly limited his preferences rather than broadening them, to the wine world’s great detriment in the opinion of many, and one with inexcusable blind spots and wine tone deafness for someone who has been given what he has been given by his supporters. He is completely ignorant with respect to Burgundy, Brunello, Port, Riesling, Loire wines of all stripes. What he knows is Bordeaux and other Cabernet-based wines and Syrah and Grenache-based wines that fall into a narrow and extreme modernist profile. Calling him out on that is hardly arrogant. It is fact easily discoverable from his body of work and ranting. By the way, until I dropped my WA subscription a year ago, I had read everything that Parker had ever put into print in Wine Advocate and book form, so I know whereof I speak on his body of work. I know how his palate developed, evolved and declined. There cannot be many people around here either old enough or masochistic enough to have done that. Same with Tanzer, Meadows, the Wine Spectator, Gambero Rosso, Gilman and at least ten other sources until I let my subscriptions lapse.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
What people tend to forget is that there have always been many consumers who preferred heavy. sweet, wines. Port and other reinforced wines were fantastically popular in the 18th and 19th centuries, much more so than today, and I think it’s not unreasonable to think that the types of people who once preferred Port are now drinking 15+ percent California wines and new-style Bordeaux. The history of blending Bordeaux over the centuries to create a richer wine is well known.

For myself, I like a wine with more refreshing and subtle qualities, and I think something is being lost in the stampede to Parkerize. But it’s more that we are having excessive homgeniety and soullessness in style than that the Parkerized style itself is so horrible. That style has always been a part of wine.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:

No, bob, Parker is an experienced taster, but one who has constantly limited his preferences rather than broadening them, to the wine world’s great detriment in the opinion of many, and one with inexcusable blind spots and wine tone deafness for someone who has been given what he has been given by his supporters.

This seems to be a natural human tendency. People have worshiped him over the years. Also, your view of blind spots and deafness, he may defend as his conviction.

Bill Klapp:

He is completely ignorant with respect to Burgundy, Brunello, Port, Riesling, Loire wines of all stripes. What he knows is Bordeaux and other Cabernet-based wines and Syrah and Grenache-based wines that fall into a narrow and extreme modernist profile.

I noticed that most wine lovers now want the critics to specialize in a few areas. Then you have to also blame Alan Meadow and other critics who specialize which you may have pointed out at the end of your post.

I also followed Bob very closely. I once worshipped him now agree on some and disagree on others. IMO, he was doing a great job in the 80s to the 90s. Even then, he definitely preferred the ripe style wines and not adverse to oak as in white Burgundy. I believe the Faiveley incident really scared him. At some point, his preference in very ripe style of wine became extreme. I still think he is doing a decent job in Bordeaux.

posts.name: David S. Corrick
posts.cooked:
Ed,

My reasoning is simply that one can not assume that every wine that achieves an initial 100 pt. rating by Parker or any evaluator will live up to the expectations that score implies in the future.

Specializing in a few areas makes sense partly because there is just so much more wine these days. In Ribera del Duero thirty years ago there were a dozen wineries. Now there are over 175. That’s a relatively small area. So how can you have a critic to cover “Spain”? And look at the proliferation of wineries across California. And Italy and France and the countries that were behind the iron curtain. One single critic can’t understand the whole wine world these days. It’s just not possible.

As far as sweet wines - people have always liked sweet wines. That’s true and it always will be. It’s a really underserved market as a matter of fact.

In any event, I am finding Bill on a real roll here - quite enjoying his writing. And even more, I’m appreciating the fact that we have well-crafted sentences and nary an elipse in sight! No “Parkerization” of Bill, that’s for damned sure.

As far as the comments regarding the expense of wine and the sophistication of tasters at various price points - I can say from having sold a fair bit of wine over the years that there is zero correlation between what people pay and what they know about wine in general and/or what they like.

The people buying those first growths and cult California wines are buying based on points - look at the secondary market to see the effect of points on the auction prices of the wines and it’s pretty clear. The only difference between those people and the folks who buy based on shelf talkers like the poster mentioned his sister asking about is that the former may know a particular critic, i.e. Parker. In the case of those buying from shelf talkers, they simply look at the points. That’s why Wine Spectator and Enthusiast are put in the same category as “Parker” even though both publications, or should I say all three publications have several individual critics who are quite fine.

It is of course true that after buying a few wines based on points one develops a fondness for some of them and then buys them in the future regardless of points, but anyone in the business will tell you that points move product. Tasting notes don’t.

This is not to bash Parker in any way - it’s not his fault that people produce wines to garner his approval. Hell, anyone would do it if it meant you could sell all your production at great profit, because the right points also move a wine’s price into a different level. The only influence WS really has is on their “wine of the year” pricing and look at what happens to the price of that wine after it gets the nod. A thirty dollar wine goes to one hundred dollars, and a hundred dollar wine goes to two hundred.

Bordeaux OTOH has always been a rigged and ranked market - before Parker came to influence in the US, Americans maybe bought based on the various rankings or the musings of Brit critics, but if you want an example of producing to a target, look at the south of France - say Gigondas and Chateauneuf du Pape, where it seems like Phillipe Cambie is producing 16% alcohol syrup for half the wineries.

It’s not necessary to do that either. There is plenty of German Riesling that has a bit of residual sugar but that is still balanced and delicious and not overly hot. Unfortunately I think Bill called it right about that love for Grenache and Cab. I happen to love both myself, but Clos Erasmus eventually got 100 points and consequently it’s several hundred dollars a bottle these days. Never tasted blind for scoring BTW, and produced by the wife of one of Bob’s favorite importers and friends. I’ve tasted most vintages of it and when tasting blind, I’ve rarely given it over 90 points. Sometimes it truly is a nice enough wine, just nothing particularly profound. But that’s perfection these days. In that region however, unlike Bordeaux, there is no real tradition to look to, so nobody complains.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
BTW, it would be helpful to list some specific examples of the traditional wines that you love. Have you had the 90 Leoville Poyferre, Margaux, Cheval Blanc, Montrose, Pichon Baron and Rauzan Segla? If so, do you like them? For me these are very opulent and ripe wines but still remain pretty youthful and will improve for at least another decade. I believe the evolution of the 09s will closely resemble them.

Let’s look at the broad scope. It is easy to stated that you don’t like Pape Clement, Pavie and Cos but these are only a few extremes. It is also important to note the vintage characteristics, the 09 and the 10 produced highly concentrated, ripe wines with high alcohol. The 11s and the 12s are in general let’s say, more “traditional” in style. There is no denying Bob’s influence and the producers desire to please him. However, I don’t think the situation is as dire as some like to portrait. My two cents.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
I agree with everything Greg said, including that Bill has been on a very enjoyable roll in this thread.

Re Chateauneuf, I do not have long experience with the region so I can’t tell if it’s just been learning my own tastes or the impact Parker has had, but I find a large amount of stuff coming out of there to be borderline undrinkable. I prefer Cotes du Rhone to those super-big CNdP alcohol bombs that get the high scores from Parker. I guess Beaucastel still has some backbone. Just in terms of being able to find stuff that is refreshing and well structured, it seems to me the situation in Bordeaux is far better than down there…but of course it’s just a better wine region (not that you would know it from Parker’s scores).

If you want to mourn Bordeaux, though, I was looking at a book on French wines published in the early 1950s, and the author says that the right bank/St. Emilion is a ‘perfect midpoint between Bordeaux and Burgundy’. He compares it to a heavier Volnay. Those days are long gone!

Bill,
I am as critical of the critics as you. I have met and tasted with enough of them.

It is easy to generate a rating formula that is almost risk free. This is what most critics do. Use the 1855 classification and add vintage factor ±3. During the EP, you get a lot of inputs, X chateau made a great wine and Y chateau had some issues, which the critics can easily incorporate to their rating system.

Regarding Bob, I just find it hard to argue with his ratings other than all bring a couple points too high. He is not any worse than any other critics.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
Kevin, that is the intended point of this exercise. And some things may be getting lost in the shuffle. For instance, there always have been and always will be ripe, opulent Bordeaux that are great wines, and that have nothing to do with Parker’s influence. To me, that is where all of the AFWE foolishness falls apart. There is pretty much universal agreement on the greatness of such wines. I have bought and enjoyed 4 of the 6 1990s that you mentioned. However, there is a huge difference between the ripe, opulent wines that nature and the vintage give us, and those that are the product of a calculated winemaking technique. Parker was a total unknown when the 1982 Bordeaux came around, for example. His reputation was made by that vintage, but he had nothing to do with its excellence. And those were those old, dirty, bretty wines that he subsequently took credit for eradicatng, eh?

And Kevin, I am not sure that “not any worse than other critics” is exactly the ringing endorsement that Big Bob would hope for!

In the same way there is likely some good in the modern wines we see today. Like in all things though it’s a matter of balance. So let the pendulum swing and we will see where we are in a few more years.

And you can see this happening in California. While there is certainly no shortage of “new world” styled wines (and I definitely see no reason for them to disappear) there has been an explosion of people making wines in styles I like. It’s a really exciting time.

I don’t see that happening in Bordeaux until after Parker retires though.

Craig Gleason:

To be fair, an awful lot of people here were also Parker Children and came to their current taste in Bordeaux through his notes. I expect a lot of people today are doing the same, and will figure out which styles they like. I figured out that a 2nd-ish growth wine that he rated 92 or 93 was likely to be right in my sweet spot. It might be tougher today since there is more divergence in style and most critics aren’t doing a very good job of pointing it out (I’m still wondering what I’ll think when I open a 98 or 00 Pape Clement… at the time it just sounded like if you liked it before, just wait until you taste the new ones!).

Raises hand. I started out as a Wine Advocate reader back in the '80s and learned a lot from Parker. I still have my first edition of his Bordeaux book (full of scores in the '70s and '80s and a rare few in the '90s). Gradually as I tasted more and discovered Burgundy and his scores veered more to the high extract, very ripe style I found his notes less and less useful and eventually useless. But I remember the days of cross referencing scores with prices so I have considerable sympathy for those who still do that

re Pape Clement - I think that '98 is the mid point of their stylistic change. I like it a lot but it’s definitely not the older version (which I also like). By 2000 it had gotten too modern for me.

And I should make it clear that I have no problem with Parker (or Jeff) liking the wines they like. My only problem has been Parker’s influence in wine making style in many regions as it meant many wines I used to like dropped off my list. But even that doesn’t really bother me now as his influence has declined so much almost everywhere outside of Bordeaux.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Kevin Shin:

BTW, it would be helpful to list some specific examples of the traditional wines that you love. Have you had the 90 Leoville Poyferre, Margaux, Cheval Blanc, Montrose, Pichon Baron and Rauzan Segla? If so, do you like them? For me these are very opulent and ripe wines but still remain pretty youthful and will improve for at least another decade. I believe the evolution of the 09s will closely resemble them.

The longevity of the 1990 vintage is simply a myth. Between 1993 and 1996 I have bought and tasted almost all Grand Cru Classé wines from the left bank, and, of course, many more. Most of them, especially Pichon Baron, are shadows today of what they was in the past, but always good enough to impress people who have not had the opportunity to drink these fantastic wines after release.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked: And BTW, a very useful mthy for high prices…

Rudi Finkler:
Kevin Shin:

BTW, it would be helpful to list some specific examples of the traditional wines that you love. Have you had the 90 Leoville Poyferre, Margaux, Cheval Blanc, Montrose, Pichon Baron and Rauzan Segla? Most of them, especially Pichon Baron, are shadows today of what they was in the past, but always good enough to impress people who have not had the opportunity to drink these fantastic wines after release.

I have had the 1990 LP, CB, and PB, mentioned by Kevin above, in the past 12-18 months. They were fantastic to my palate, and do concur that they will continue to improve. If that is what the 09s will resemble than I think the buyers of that vintage will be very happy.

Rudi,

I did not try the 90s in their youth, was not legally possible . As such, I am quite taken aback that they were even better in their youth.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:
Kevin Shin:

BTW, it would be helpful to list some specific examples of the traditional wines that you love. Have you had the 90 Leoville Poyferre, Margaux, Cheval Blanc, Montrose, Pichon Baron and Rauzan Segla? If so, do you like them? For me these are very opulent and ripe wines but still remain pretty youthful and will improve for at least another decade. I believe the evolution of the 09s will closely resemble them.

The longevity of the 1990 vintage is simply a myth. Between 1993 and 1996 I have bought and tasted almost all Grand Cru Classé wines from the left bank, and, of course, many more. Most of them, especially Pichon Baron, are shadows today of what they was in the past, but always good enough to impress people who have not had the opportunity to drink these fantastic wines after release.

Are you saying that you bought and tasted the 90s between 1993 and 1996? If not when was the peak for the 90 Pichon Baron?

My impression of the 90s tasted this year.

  • 1990 Château Margaux - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Margaux (9/30/2013)
    A small impromptu gathering at The Capital Grille – 85 La La, 61 HB, 52 CB, 90 Margaux and etc. (The Capital Grille at Tysons corner, VA): One of the most consistent wines for me. Big, ripe and intense sweet fruits that clearly represent the vintage character yet complex, youthful and polished that represent the Margaux terroir. Expressive hedonistic nose displaying cassis, blackberry jam and lead pencil. Incredible concentration yet incredibly polished. Still extremely young yet drinking perfectly with beautiful intense young jammy fruits. (100 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Figeac - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, St. Émilion Grand Cru (1/22/2013)
    An impromptu gathering at Adam’s house (Washington DC): This particular bottle displays heavily cab franc driven green nose that is not bothersome. Black fruits, caramel and mint. Sweet dark fruits, medium concentration and airy silky palate. Lovely wine that will go well with food. (92 pts.)
  • 1990 Château La Conseillante - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, Pomerol (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 1 - This is the most delicate and lightest red of the night, red fruits, chocolate, mint, sweet spices and anise. Seamless silky palate, very cool impression and a nice clean finish. First guessed Lynch Bages but after tasting all nine, this has to be La Conseillant. (95 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Beausejour (Duffau Lagarrosse) - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, St. Émilion Grand Cru (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 2 - Ripe, sexy, blue fruits, lactic and chocolate. Big scale merlot based wine. With air, a hint of pencil and noticeable tannins. Initially thinking La Conseillante then ended up Léoville-Las Cases.

I mentioned to Assaf that this should not be decanted as at this stage, pop and pour is the way to show the opulent exotic sexy fruit. Assaf agree that with a couple hours of air, the wine lost the magic. I highly recommend pop and pour on this and the 90 La Conseillante. (95 pts.)

  • 1990 Château Troplong Mondot - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, St. Émilion Grand Cru (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 3 - Once again no great wines only great bottles. The last bottle had less than a year ago was very port like. This on the other hand showed beautifully. Explosive sexy nose, silky, big, ripe, caramel, cool fruit and earth. Big scale wine with excellent concentration, silky palate and long finish. The caramel note and sweet palate lead me to think Leoville Poyferre. (97 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Léoville Las Cases - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Julien (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 4 – earth, lead pencil, mint, plume and eucalyptus. Lean fruit expression, polished silky palate and nicely integrated tannins. Lynch Bages. (95 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Léoville Poyferré - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Julien (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Another big scale ripe wine with a caramel note, also cedar, lactic and cassis. Excellent concentration, chewy and silky palate and long finish. (97 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Lynch-Bages - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 6 - Lead pencil, ash, cassis, cedar and earth. A hint of brett I thought and the power of suggestion kicked in. Thought Montrose and never look back. Assaf thought just way to young. (90 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Latour Grand Vin - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Medium expressive yet very youthful nose displaying dark fruits, lead pencil, cassis and earth. The palate is noble, not big, impressive and expressive like the 90 Margaux or Cheval Blan, but seamless, airy and posses the perfect balance. Reminds me a great Musigny, not big, not sweet but cool and suave. Latour. (98 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Pichon-Longueville Baron - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 8 - Intense cassis, a hint of scorched earth, lead pencil, menthol and eucalyptus. Cool palate impression, sweet fruit, excellent concentration and silky palate. Pichon Baron. (96 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Montrose - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, St. Estèphe (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): This is the most intense, expressive and concentrated wine of the night. Cassis, dark fruits, caramel and bitter chocolate. Intensely sweet and chewy palate, excellent concentration, no noticeable tannins and long finish. Flirting with perfection. Beausejour Duffau. (99 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:
Kevin Shin:

BTW, it would be helpful to list some specific examples of the traditional wines that you love. Have you had the 90 Leoville Poyferre, Margaux, Cheval Blanc, Montrose, Pichon Baron and Rauzan Segla? If so, do you like them? For me these are very opulent and ripe wines but still remain pretty youthful and will improve for at least another decade. I believe the evolution of the 09s will closely resemble them.

The longevity of the 1990 vintage is simply a myth. Between 1993 and 1996 I have bought and tasted almost all Grand Cru Classé wines from the left bank, and, of course, many more. Most of them, especially Pichon Baron, are shadows today of what they was in the past, but always good enough to impress people who have not had the opportunity to drink these fantastic wines after release.

Not sure I agree here on the vintage as a whole. While I didn’t lay any down this year I’ve been fortunate enough to try the 1990 Ducru (wonderful and just starting to come into its own with a lot of air), Leoville Las Cases (fantastic, seems to be in prime drinking stage), and Pichon Baron (very, very good though not quite as good as the other two), I didn’t try the PB on release so you may be right about it having been better then (I certainly feel that’s true of the 1988 and 1989 PBs though I can’t rule out palate shifts as a cause) but I’m not sure it can be generalized to the entire vintage.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked: If Rudi liked the PB better between the 93 and the 96, he should be a major proponent of the modernist movement. Just saying!

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Both Neal and Bob seem to like the wine. Neal wrote “If you do not like the Pichon Baron 1990, you do not like Bordeaux. Tasted November 2012.” The recent CT notes are very positive, mostly refer the wine as young.
My notes;

  • 1990 Château Pichon-Longueville Baron - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (1/18/2013)
    1990 Bordeaux dinner with Latour, Beausejour Duffau, Montrose and etc (Capital Grille Tysons, VA): Wine no 8 - Intense cassis, a hint of scorched earth, lead pencil, menthol and eucalyptus. Cool palate impression, sweet fruit, excellent concentration and silky palate. Pichon Baron. (96 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Pichon-Longueville Baron - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (5/3/2012)
    Bernard Burtschy dinner at Taberna del Alabardero (Taberna del Alabardero, Washington D.C.): This is a great example of the 90 Pauillac. Very ripe, dark fruit expression is not unlike the 90 Leoville Poyferre. Sweet spices, licorice, ink, caramel and cedar. Silky palate. I preferred the hedonistic aspect of this wine to the complex nose of the 90 La Mission. (97 pts.)
  • 1990 Château Pichon-Longueville Baron - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Pauillac (9/27/2011)
    Mature Classified Growth Bordeaux gathering at Lavandou (Washington DC): I love the 90 PB and it has been extremely consistent without much bottle variation. This is a big scale very ripe wine. A classic Pauillac nose of cassis, ink, perfume and lead pencil. The palate displays ripe dark fruit, impressive concentration and long finish. Having very ripe hedonistic fruits, some may say this is Californian but I would say a typical 90 left bank Bordeaux. Although incredible, perhaps I prefer the 90 Leoville Poyferre that I had recently which I found more hedonistic. (97 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Kevin Shin:

If Rudi liked the PB better between the 93 and the 96, he should be a major proponent of the modernist movement. Just saying!

Old or young, that’s not important. Elegance, delicacy and balance are the magical words, Kevin.

BTW, I`m not interested in Bob’s or Neal’s likes or dislikes…

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I bought, tried, and loved, some profound Bdx from the '80s before I really ever knew who Parker was. Talbot. Gruard Larose. Lynch Bages. Pichon Baron. Montrose. Pichon Lalande. Moved me enough to spend part of my honeymoon in Bdx in 1996. Those are still benchmark wines for me, and funny enough, over the last few years, guess what I have been backfilling? The '82 and '86 Talbots and Gruard Laroses that I popped this past year were tremendous, as was some '82 La Lagune, GPL and Sociando Mallet.

I admit to having some modern stuff in my cellar. Some of it is actually quite decent (2005 La Vieille Cure, for example). Not what I reach for first when I want a moving experience, and I wonder how it will mature, but it is part of the learning experience.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
