Kevin Shin:

If Rudi liked the PB better between the 93 and the 96, he should be a major proponent of the modernist movement. Just saying!

Old or young, that’s not important. Elegance, delicacy and balance are the magical words, Kevin.

BTW, I`m not interested in Bob’s or Neal’s likes or dislikes…

I was just pointing out that most views contrast yours. I don’t think you would have found elegance and delicacy in the 90 PB.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Kevin Shin:

… I don’t think you would have found elegance and delicacy in the 90 PB.

Have I said so?

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:

The longevity of the 1990 vintage is simply a myth. Between 1993 and 1996 I have bought and tasted almost all Grand Cru Classé wines from the left bank, and, of course, many more. Most of them, especially Pichon Baron, are shadows today of what they was in the past, but always good enough to impress people who have not had the opportunity to drink these fantastic wines after release.

To each his own. But I do not agree. I bought all the top and mid level 1990 Bordeaux on release as did most of my friends and get to taste them often from various cellars with different storage conditions. Most are fresh, vibrant and youthful today. Pichon Baron that you mentioned. IMO gets better year after year. I think that wine is a rockstar Bordeaux that belongs in any BDX lovers cellar. I have a lot of it and wish I had more. That is a wine to grow old with. I have 12 tasting notes taken over the past 9 years and IMO, that wine gets better with each tasting. Learn about Chateau Pichon Baron Pauillac, Complete Guide

link_counts.title: Learn about Chateau Pichon Baron Pauillac, Complete Guide

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked: To each his own, as you said…

Bill Klapp:

… And many now suffer arrested development and a myopic view of wine as a consequence. They dig in their heels like Jeff Leve, John Gonzales and others and vigorously defend the right to be myopic, rather than making the effort to broaden their horizons. Preferences are one thing; sticking one’s head in the sand quite another… just spouts off in an effort to stifle the dialogue of intelligent and articulate people whose opinions appear to make him insecure…

Bill, fait enough about not posting yet in this threead. I actually have formulated at least three replies but they’ve rambled to the extent that I just saved the drafts. At the same time what I consider pontificating grows. I just don’t care for your condescending nature. Trust me, many people of a differing view than yours don’t waste their time.

I would say that your reply to me are also illustrative of a general trend in this discussion. You make judgements and assumptions about me, just as you do about a group of people that like and defend modern wines. I’ve never suscribed to the Wine Advocate, I don’t consider myself a child of Parker. From being on the boards all these years, I like everyone see most of his scores. But I’m 53 years old, and have consumed a lot of wine. I’ve been on these wine boards for 15+ years and have at least 25 books on wine. I drink and buy wine of many types. I would venture to guess, since I have a wife in the wine business as well as my own interests, that I taste as many or more wines of all sorts than you do. I drink a good amount of Bordeaux. Again, very likely as much as you. For curiosity’s sake, how much bordeaux do you even buy, even of the traditional variety? While on a vintage-in-vintage-out basis I prefer what most would consider modern, I drink a lot of what is considered traditional. But with whatever amount of qualification or experience one would conider that, my likes in Bordeaux are really only applicable to me. Also, very important to my opinion is that in addition to drinking wine with a lot of people on this board, I go to events and drink with people that are not quite as obsessed. Doing so gives one a perspective on what the broad-based world of wine drinkers really is.

The point is: How would you know for a second that I am myopic, what information I’ve assimilated, or what “horizons” I’ve considered. You don’t. What you know is that many people disagree with your view. As you typically do, you make assumptions about those that simply disagree with you. In this discussion you must resort to that because we all know that your preferences are the minority. You make qualifications about a market that clearly supports another point of view. So you have to resort to elavating your view as “intelligent” and proclaim to teach those who are less enlightened. Or you make unsubstantiated claims that anyone using a score as a data point lacks any amount of independent analysis.

There is a huge spectrum of wine drinkers out there. Some have a lot of books and a lot of experience. Some have no books and are new to the pursuit. Then there is a huge spectrum in the middle. All the intelligent and experienced people don’t populate one end of the traditional vs. modern spectrum. But for that matter, if Joe with the 50 bottle collection buys a case of Pavie while you hate it, his opinion is just as valid as yours. Especially as it applies to where it most important, himself. To the extent that he actually participates more in the Bordeaux market than you, some would say he is more important. Even if you’re more intelligant, and type more words on a wine board. In the end it all gets returned to the fact that we each have our own preferences, and we are talking about a subjectively appreciated product.

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:

Thanks for your thoughtful post! The postulation is that winemakers and their consultants find their way back to the essential values elegance and balance. This is a universal claim, but not a right, of course. From this perspective a refocusing on these values is inevitable. But the banal question remains: When will they do it? In a hundred years?

Not only when will they do it, but where will they do it? The answer is that I don’t know, but I personally believe that it could come from an unlikely place. Perhaps it’s a subregion in Australia or California? I could see it being somewhere on the California central coast. I don’t think that “modern”, or essentially “primary” style wines will ever disappear because they offer a visceral level of pleasure and there will always be an audience for that. However it is part of the human experience to seek greater meaning in things and at some point we will come full-circle towards wine with real “substance”. I assume this will likely not come from consumer demand, but from the winemakers who grow weary.

Bill Klapp:

No, Bob, deciding to further one’s wine education and broaden one’s tasting experience is the personal choice and personal preference. Once one decides to do that, the “educated taste” is a fact and the logical consequence of the educational process. What any given person thinks of any given wine is indeed subjective, but, over time, wine drinkers build consensus on which of the world’s wines are the best, best for the money, or whatever other yardstick may be used from time to time. And, like it or not, that process results in something not far from objectivity. Not one person in a million could taste a pristine bottle of 1990 La Tache or 1989 Haut-Brion blind and not be blown away by the experience. I submit to you that this would be true even for those who do not like Burgundy or Bordeaux. (I use obvious, easy examples; there are many less rare and less expensive wines about which I could make the same point.)

The problem arises when the consensus of experienced wine drinkers butts heads with Parker and the rest of the wine-reviewing community. Parker’s opinion is one; the experienced are many, and Parker was sucking down Coca-Cola when he decided that wine was pretty good stuff. Parker has become arrogant, close-minded and self-righteous in his views; the experienced often vigorously debate one another, rarely afraid to disagree, using the dialectic to learn even more. Parker and his ilk pretend to be consumer’s advocates, but in fact, end up doing little more than moving product, even getting medals from governments of wine-producing countries in appreciation of all the product that got moved; the experienced just drink the stuff. Parker and his ilk taste 10,000 wines a year, spending seconds or minutes with most of them, and then presume to render a trite, inarticulate Cliff’s Notes judgment on the wine, complete with points from a scale that is demonstrable bullshit, both in concept and application; the experienced often spend hours or days with a single bottle, years or decades tasting a single wine repeatedly, for their own education and enjoyment. Over time, Parker’s palate has become immune to oak and tannins, and he appears to be incapable of appreciating anything but fruit bombs that have been made to order for him. His palate, never much to begin with, is shot. He needs to retire with what little grace and dignity he can muster.

That taste in wine is subjective answers nothing, and is not the beginning nor the end of the story. Too many people with more disposable income than taste or judgment pick up the Wine Advocate or Wine Spectator and rush out to buy wines that they have never tasted because Parker or Suckling dealt the wines scores of 96-100. (Admittedly, one has to start somewhere, so I cannot fault a person for succumbing to the reviewers’ siren songs in the absence of other available information.) After a while, they have cellars and storage lockers full of the same wine. It is what they prefer because it is all that they know. And the reviewers constantly churn out the big scores for the same wines, with only the vintages changing the numbers slightly. It is wine’s answer to a hamster wheel, not the settling on a preferred style after years of experiencing thousands of different wines. Most of the people who are violently anti-Burgundy know nothing about Burgundy. They do not drink it because Parker does not drink or understand it, and he has told the sheep (giving lemmings a rest for a moment) that Burgundy is a minefield of shrill, green, acidic wines. (Of course , all of the old Parker 96-100s on Burgundies would seem to make him a short-sighted BSer on that count. He was never able to figure out that Burgundy is an extremely ageworthy wine, and that the best rarely reveal their extraordinary qualities without many years in the bottle.) Maybe the occasional sheep will seek out a few bottles of Burgundy, do no homework, drink it too young while dumb, have a “Bob is right” a-ha moment and go back to drinking Cabernet, Syrah and Grenache-based fruit bombs. That, Bob, is the reality that we confront. The experienced rarely, if ever, lock themselves into a single wine or style. We have many people on this board that drink nothing but high-octane California wines, or those and high-octane wines from Bordeaux and the rest of the world. That does make their palates and opinions count for less. They are entitled to like what they like, but must do so in the midst of those who know not only such wines, but thousands of others as well. It is a real pity that those who know less are so often resentful of those who know more, and can do nothing but attempt to discredit superior knowledge. Make no mistake: this is rarely, if ever, about superior noses and palates…it is about superior knowledge and experience. “Completely subjective” is a cop-out, not a viable argument.

Bill, I think you’ve hit on a number of critical points but I don’t believe that they apply only to Robert Parker or any prominent wine taster. Instead, I believe you are speaking to knowledge in general (beyond even wine). It is not exclusive to any individual or group because it is the nature of seeking truth/wisdom.

You speak of the dialectic; so do you not believe that those who are entrenched in traditional wines are not influenced by group-think? Aren’t there those (including here on WB) who carry along tasting traditional wines with the belief that it is what they “should” like, despite not reaching it through some type of personal, intellectual pursuit? There is no intellectual integrity in either position, old or new world if it is not born of some type of personal experience or personal pursuit for meaning. Even if you believe they are right in whatever position they take, it’s hardly good company in the end.

We have all seen those who bow to the pressure of reputation or perceived “consensus” in wine. Has anyone here not seen Grand Cru Burgundy show poorly yet looked across the room or table and see some fellow taster wax poetically about it all the same? This is a pattern of human behavior, aside from Parker and the wine critics. There is no respite, regardless of what side you are on, from this type of behavior (And we are all victim of it at some time, I’m sure!). The pursuit for truth, knowledge and authenticity is a decidedly personal one with few to walk alongside with, even in wine.

I would even state (as I have before) that there is some good in modern wines. Clearly it is not all the same, but there are “profound” elements in some of these wines. They are not to be abandoned, but rather married with other characteristics to create some type of larger, better whole. Intense primary/fruit elements are not in their own sake things to be avoided. When paired with other complementary elements of place (terroir) and varietal notes it can become something incredible. In this sense, neither is to be abandoned but rather the pursuit of both like a rising tide that raises all (in all things, balance!). Besides, not all that is seemingly intellectual is best. Isn’t the basis for catharsis both an intellectual and emotional moment? In the same way some of the most profound elements in wine are the marriage of the visceral and the intellectual. I do believe that there are wonderful elements that are learned, but the transcendent wines should reach across these boundaries.

This isn’t to say that you disagree with that statement nor is it a refutation but rather a re-statement that I think that is the basis of your argument - balance. However it is not entirely one side (the traditional) versus the other (the modern). In both cases we have lack of balance and there are many intellectual wines that lack some of the visceral pleasure that might elevate it to another level. In the same way, we have modern wines that seem virtually exhausting because it appeals to only one element.

TBroussard - great post. Intelligent and thoughtful.

WTF?

Be careful with stuff like this though:

so do you not believe that those who are entrenched in traditional wines are not influenced by group-think? Aren’t there those (including here on WB) who carry along tasting traditional wines with the belief that it is what they “should” like

I got slammed for suggesting that. And to be fair to those who were offended, we don’t know what’s in someone else’s head.

Unfortunately, you can actually observe the group-think taking hold, and not only in wine. One of the clearest examples I ever saw was on my very first DAY of law school where there were a handful of people who just seemed so anxious to show that he or she “thought like an attorney”. Was quite weird.

Having seen people enter the wine business and rapidly adopt a particular stance when they were open to all experiences just weeks earlier, I’m certain that there’s a large element of group-think at work. It doesn’t really matter what type of wine one prefers either, there are groups for that preference.

Anyway, good post and your last paragraph was great (other than the fact that the final pronoun didn’t agree with its antecedent).

Cheers!

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
TBroussard:
Rudi Finkler:

Thanks for your thoughtful post! The postulation is that winemakers and their consultants find their way back to the essential values elegance and balance. This is a universal claim, but not a right, of course. From this perspective a refocusing on these values is inevitable. But the banal question remains: When will they do it? In a hundred years?

Not only when will they do it, but where will they do it? The answer is that I don’t know, but I personally believe that it could come from an unlikely place. Perhaps it’s a subregion in Australia or California? I could see it being somewhere on the California central coast. I don’t think that “modern”, or essentially “primary” style wines will ever disappear because they offer a visceral level of pleasure and there will always be an audience for that. However it is part of the human experience to seek greater meaning in things and at some point we will come full-circle towards wine with real “substance”. I assume this will likely not come from consumer demand, but from the winemakers who grow weary.

Bill Klapp:

No, Bob, deciding to further one’s wine education and broaden one’s tasting experience is the personal choice and personal preference. Once one decides to do that, the “educated taste” is a fact and the logical consequence of the educational process. What any given person thinks of any given wine is indeed subjective, but, over time, wine drinkers build consensus on which of the world’s wines are the best, best for the money, or whatever other yardstick may be used from time to time. And, like it or not, that process results in something not far from objectivity. Not one person in a million could taste a pristine bottle of 1990 La Tache or 1989 Haut-Brion blind and not be blown away by the experience. I submit to you that this would be true even for those who do not like Burgundy or Bordeaux. (I use obvious, easy examples; there are many less rare and less expensive wines about which I could make the same point.)

The problem arises when the consensus of experienced wine drinkers butts heads with Parker and the rest of the wine-reviewing community. Parker’s opinion is one; the experienced are many, and Parker was sucking down Coca-Cola when he decided that wine was pretty good stuff. Parker has become arrogant, close-minded and self-righteous in his views; the experienced often vigorously debate one another, rarely afraid to disagree, using the dialectic to learn even more. We have many people on this board that drink nothing but high-octane California wines, or those and high-octane wines from Bordeaux and the rest of the world. Make no mistake: this is rarely, if ever, about superior noses and palates…it is about superior knowledge and experience. “Completely subjective” is a cop-out, not a viable argument.

Bill, I think you’ve hit on a number of critical points but I don’t believe that they apply only to Robert Parker or any prominent wine taster. Instead, I believe you are speaking to knowledge in general (beyond even wine). It is not exclusive to any individual or group because it is the nature of seeking truth/wisdom.

You speak of the dialectic; so do you not believe that those who are entrenched in traditional wines are not influenced by group-think? Aren’t there those (including here on WB) who carry along tasting traditional wines with the belief that it is what they “should” like, despite not reaching it through some type of personal, intellectual pursuit? This is a pattern of human behavior, aside from Parker and the wine critics. There is no respite, regardless of what side you are on, from this type of behavior (And we are all victim of it at some time, I’m sure!). I would even state (as I have before) that there is some good in modern wines. Clearly it is not all the same, but there are “profound” elements in some of these wines. They are not to be abandoned, but rather married with other characteristics to create some type of larger, better whole. Intense primary/fruit elements are not in their own sake things to be avoided. However it is not entirely one side (the traditional) versus the other (the modern). In both cases we have lack of balance and there are many intellectual wines that lack some of the visceral pleasure that might elevate it to another level. In the same way, we have modern wines that seem virtually exhausting because it appeals to only one element.

TBroussard, here is what I think:

Absolutely, what I said applies to wine reviewers, and knowledge, generally. As to the “dialectic” among the “experienced”, as I shorthanded above, yes, of course there is an element of group-think. The natural wine movement, which does have some important points to make, is dominantly blind group-think, led by shooting-from-the-hip buffoons like Alice Feiring (the Parker of the politically correct), and the worse for it. However, listening to that camp rail at Parker and vice versa is nothing more than group-think tanks at war. However, when I think of the experienced with a traditional bent, and especially those on this thread, I do not see black-and-white group-think to the radical extent that I see it among Parkerites. Part of the reason is no doubt because there are few traditionalist leaders, demogogues and gurus to foster group-think. Gilman, perhaps, but (sorry, John!) his influence is quite limited. Guys like Meadows and Galloni (who has serious experience limitations to begin with) are not really traditionalists. In the modernist camp, we have Parker, many of his present and former hired help (Jeb Dunnuck apparently cloned using Parker stem cells), Suckling, most of the WS crowd, most of the Gambero Rosso crowd, Rolland, Cambie, Cotarella, Bettane, De Grazia and other intermeddling importers, and many more, in many cases carrying on bromances within the group, like Parker’s with Rolland and Cotarella. What I am saying is that, while group-think may be endemic to humanity, you do not find it in an institutionalized and radical self-promoting form as you do modernists. I am also saying that you will often see experienced traditionalists having at each other over a given wine or issue, while you generally observe Parkerites patting each other on the back and mutually reinforcing the genius of having stocked up on 100-pointers. There are myriad studies suggesting the huge impact of power of suggestion when it comes to wine (reds mistaken for whites and vice versa in one famous study), and it is clear that the palate can be trained and conditioned if one chooses to do so. And yes, there is ego-and insecurity-driven behavior everywhere in the wine world (your Burgundy showing poorly above, but also Parker and the other 1997 Harlan VA-deniers, eh?), but I find that experienced, knowledgable wine drinkers are far more likely to throw in the towel on a flawed bottle and move on than a newbie who has just heard others denigate what was the most expensive bottle in his or her cellar. Again, broad experience teaches one to identify closed or flawed wines and to learn from the disappointing experience.

Also, while I surely have not emphasized it here, clearly neither Parker nor any other reviewer gets it wrong all of the time, and it is true that there are some good modernist wines and lame traditional wines. The truly experienced will tend to give all wines an even shake, and are unlikely to champion traditionalism for its own sake, regardless of how the wines actually taste. There are no doubt some individuals who will mouth one-dimensional, reactionary and unsupportable traditionalist views. however, there is no loud-mouthed, self-righteous, self-validating traditionalist movement (well, maybe excepting Alice Feiring!) to compare to what has gone on in the past three decades with the above-named reviewers, consultants and importers, and no sweeping conversion of modernist fruit bombs back to traditionally made wines (although hopefully that is on its way as the pendulum swings back in many wine regions), and no significant and tragic damage done to the modernist style, so I will have to reject any notion that the traditionalist and modernist camps are but equal and opposite sides of the same coin, with all of the same strengths and shortcomings. Available evidence will not even remotely support that. The wine styles, and the wines themselves, can, however, meet in the middle and achieve some element of balance and harmony. It just has not happened yet, and I think that is due to the extremity of the formulaic modernist style.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
johngonzales:
Bill Klapp:

… And many now suffer arrested development and a myopic view of wine as a consequence. They dig in their heels like Jeff Leve, John Gonzales and others and vigorously defend the right to be myopic, rather than making the effort to broaden their horizons. Preferences are one thing; sticking one’s head in the sand quite another… just spouts off in an effort to stifle the dialogue of intelligent and articulate people whose opinions appear to make him insecure…

Bill, fait enough about not posting yet in this threead. I actually have formulated at least three replies but they’ve rambled to the extent that I just saved the drafts. At the same time what I consider pontificating grows. I just don’t care for your condescending nature. Trust me, many people of a differing view than yours don’t waste their time.

I would say that your reply to me are also illustrative of a general trend in this discussion. You make judgements and assumptions about me, just as you do about a group of people that like and defend modern wines. I’ve never suscribed to the Wine Advocate, I don’t consider myself a child of Parker. From being on the boards all these years, I like everyone see most of his scores. But I’m 53 years old, and have consumed a lot of wine. I’ve been on these wine boards for 15+ years and have at least 25 books on wine. I drink and buy wine of many types. I would venture to guess, since I have a wife in the wine business as well as my own interests, that I taste as many or more wines of all sorts than you do. I drink a good amount of Bordeaux. Again, very likely as much as you. For curiosity’s sake, how much bordeaux do you even buy, even of the traditional variety? While on a vintage-in-vintage-out basis I prefer what most would consider modern, I drink a lot of what is considered traditional. But with whatever amount of qualification or experience one would conider that, my likes in Bordeaux are really only applicable to me. Also, very important to my opinion is that in addition to drinking wine with a lot of people on this board, I go to events and drink with people that are not quite as obsessed. Doing so gives one a perspective on what the broad-based world of wine drinkers really is.

The point is: How would you know for a second that I am myopic, what information I’ve assimilated, or what “horizons” I’ve considered. You don’t. What you know is that many people disagree with your view. As you typically do, you make assumptions about those that simply disagree with you. In this discussion you must resort to that because we all know that your preferences are the minority. You make qualifications about a market that clearly supports another point of view. So you have to resort to elavating your view as “intelligent” and proclaim to teach those who are less enlightened. Or you make unsubstantiated claims that anyone using a score as a data point lacks any amount of independent analysis.

There is a huge spectrum of wine drinkers out there. Some have a lot of books and a lot of experience. Some have no books and are new to the pursuit. Then there is a huge spectrum in the middle. All the intelligent and experienced people don’t populate one end of the traditional vs. modern spectrum. But for that matter, if Joe with the 50 bottle collection buys a case of Pavie while you hate it, his opinion is just as valid as yours. Especially as it applies to where it most important, himself. To the extent that he actually participates more in the Bordeaux market than you, some would say he is more important. Even if you’re more intelligant, and type more words on a wine board. In the end it all gets returned to the fact that we each have our own preferences, and we are talking about a subjectively appreciated product.

John, a couple of thoughts about this. First, unless people know each other personally offline, all that can ever be known in a forum like this is what people say online, and what is said is fair game for parsing, analysis, praise and criticism. Judgments will inevitably be made, and, fairly or not, assumptions about the unknown extrapolated from the known. By invoking all of the nameless, faceless, countless people that disagree with me (not to mention those with names and faces who can be counted), including those who choose not to waste their time here, you are doing the same thing. It is human nature. You claim that “we all know that your preferences are in the minority”. Who is “we”? Certainly not most of the people on this thread. And how could you possibly know what the majority thinks? Do the silent majority just not speak up? Should the majority include soccer moms whose knowledge of wine comes exclusively from supermarket shelf-talkers? I think not. You are offering pure BS in the attempt to build a shout-down concensus against somebody that you do not agree with, John, and I am calling you out on it. (I can build that concensus without any help, and more efficiently, eh? )

As to presumptions made about you, for all that I know, you could have a 10,000-bottle cellar of traditional, old first-growth Bordeaux that you enjoy nightly. If that were true, however, nothing in your prior posts would have indicated that. (By the way, what you have posted of your own experience here is, in fact, helpful.). It is not unfair to assume that you are of the modernist bent because of your vigorous objection to my anti-modernist bent. It matters not whether you are Parker’s first cousin or whether you have never heard of him in that context, so if you feel that you have been unfairly lumped in with lemmings and sheep, I understand that. However, you need to take solace in the fact that you know what your personal history is on that count. But let us be clear about this: I am not about to tailor my ideas to accommodate unknown personal histories of other posters. You do not like my style, fine. You think that I am condescending…I understand that, from your perspective. I understand that others no doubt share your negative perspective on my style. (I can name the names for you!) However, I would think that you would understand by now that you will not stop me, or even slow me down, by attacking my style. And you will not impress others by doing that, either.

I care about ideas. I am not here to win friends and influence people. The proper response to an idea I put forth is not “what condescension and pontification!”, even if I am being both condescending and pontificating at the time (and I have been guilty of both). The proper response is to address my idea head-on and demonstrate how and why you think that it is wrong. I am not always right, and I obviously enjoy vigorous debate. I sometimes stake out positions in black and white, knowing full well that there are shades of grey. I enjoy watching others pick up on that and paint the shades of grey, even if I end up painted into the corner now and again.

I draw a distinction between the undue influence of wine reviewers who, inadvertently or not, cause winemakers to alter their style and techniques to suit high-profile but amateurish and suspect palates, and those who drink the wines thus produced. The latter are entitled to drink whatever they like, to be sure. However, for me, there are too many in that camp who never progress beyond it. Easy-drinking, early-drinking, easy-to-understand, crowd-pleasing wines are not an inherently bad thing, whether traditional or modern. I drink many myself. The problem comes when unknowledgable wine drinkers deify a snake-oil salesman like Bob Parker, thus allowing his shoot-from-the-hip, top-of-the-head judgments to dictate changes in style in a few important winemaking regions. Even worse, I do not find Parker to be possessed of adequate intelligence and focus to realize the inconsistencies in his work over time, the harm that he has caused and how increasingly political and close-minded he becomes with each passing year.

Finally, it should be said that I am defending a winemaking approach that has been under siege, not my personal preferences or experience. Ironically, perhaps, my cellar at this point in my life is well over half Italian, and that overwhelmingly Nebbiolo, which is admittedly one of the most difficult grapes on earth for new wine drinkers to understand. I would never look down on someone who does not like Barolo, nor am I inclined to push traditional Barolo and Barbaresco, my first love, upon others. Those wines come from an area that was among the first to experience modernist winemaking, NOT driven by Parker, and an area in which virtually all of the excesses and shortcomings of the modernist style, as well as its virtues, are on display. A number of modernist producers can be found in my cellar, although the aggregate number of bottles from those producers is relatively small. The rest of my cellar is eclectic, with wines from virtually all of the important wine regions. Those wines are a mix of traditional and modern, but tradition dominates. Thus, I am attacking what I see as a pervasive, harmful and short-sighted winemaking philosophy and those who propagate it, rather than those who drink the products of that dynamic. However, I do believe that, historically, the majority of true students of fine wine, those that become the “experienced” that I speak of above, all go through a rite of passage from the simple to the complex, the easy to the more difficult, the industrial to the artisanal, and yes, by and large, at least for Parker’s contemporaries and younger, from modernist to traditional. If I believe that, and I do fervently, then there wil be in my musings some collateral damage to those whom I see as suffering arrested development in their exploration of fine wine…

posts.cooked: That uses up my word quota for today. Time to visit the truffle man!

So a question for the people that are defending modern wines, do you feel that the traits of the modern wines not obscure location? Or that it doesn’t matter?

“Modern” has nothing to do with location. Why would one think that?

As Bill said above, some people give all wines a fair shake, and I think that’s notwithstanding whether the wine is placed into a “modern” or “traditional” camp by other people, or even by oneself.

Brian Loring makes some big huge Pinot Noirs in California. I’m not a big fan of them but I get what he’s shooting for and sure don’t think those wines could be made in somewhere like Burgenland or Burgundy. They are most definitely of a place.

posts.name: Gary York
posts.cooked:
So you are saying that it doesn’t matter that the wines are:
1 high in Alc
2 dominated by new oak
3 ripe to the point of jam
4 extracted to the point of being thick
5 lack acidity

Wines that have the above profile seem to lack a sense of place. And most people, not all, seem to say that they want a wine to speak of place.

posts.name: Ed Murray
posts.cooked: It amazes me how much people have to say on this subject. There are groups of people advocating each “style”, some advocating both, some advocating none. If following the herd makes you happy, find your herd and get in line. If you’re inclined to be an individual, (as most people on this board seem to be), drink what you like, like what you drink. Traditional? Modern? Who cares! If you like it - drink it. You don’t need to justify your choice, at least not to me. I won’t judge you and I don’t really care if you judge me.

Sure, I can see how place affects the wine: Vosne is different than Volnay blah blah blah… I want to drink the wine, not the place. Shitty wine from a great place is still shitty wine. Great wine from a shitty place is still great wine. Give me the wine ( but please, no Pinotage.)

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:

Thanks again for your thoughtful statement, TBroussard!

I have little to complain about it, but I add for consideration that it is rather risky to suggest vague suppositions like group-thinking and a lack of integrity without naming names and analysing facts when it comes to participants of this thread. In doing so, you potentially damage your own integrity, or at least your credibility.

I’m convinced that the vast majority of participants here are persons of integrity - on both camps. BTW, having no integrity is a conscious decision and presupposes a certain degree of malice.

Oh, I should clarify that I’m speaking only in a global sense. My words were not to be an indictment of anyone here as I couldn’t presume to know anyone well enough to render that type of judgment. (and even if I did, I don’t think it would be fair or right to say it openly). I am only saying that they are likely here, just as they are everywhere.

To further the discussion, I don’t think that there is necessarily a degree of malice. A degree of naïveté, yes. A desire for belonging, absolutely. Only when it is used as a means to divide, and not to belong would I presume that a degree of malice exists.

Bill Klapp:

TBroussard, here is what I think:

Absolutely, what I said applies to wine reviewers, and knowledge, generally. As to the “dialectic” among the “experienced”, as I shorthanded above, yes, of course there is an element of group-think. The natural wine movement, which does have some important points to make, is dominantly blind group-think, led by shooting-from-the-hip buffoons like Alice Feiring (the Parker of the politically correct), and the worse for it. However, listening to that camp rail at Parker and vice versa is nothing more than group-think tanks at war. However, when I think of the experienced with a traditional bent, and especially those on this thread, I do not see black-and-white group-think to the radical extent that I see it among Parkerites. Part of the reason is no doubt because there are few traditionalist leaders, demogogues and gurus to foster group-think. Gilman, perhaps, but (sorry, John!) his influence is quite limited. Guys like Meadows and Galloni (who has serious experience limitations to begin with) are not really traditionalists. In the modernist camp, we have Parker, many of his present and former hired help (Jeb Dunnuck apparently cloned using Parker stem cells), Suckling, most of the WS crowd, most of the Gambero Rosso crowd, Rolland, Cambie, Cotarella, Bettane, De Grazia and other intermeddling importers, and many more, in many cases carrying on bromances within the group, like Parker’s with Rolland and Cotarella. What I am saying is that, while group-think may be endemic to humanity, you do not find it in an institutionalized and radical self-promoting form as you do modernists. I am also saying that you will often see experienced traditionalists having at each other over a given wine or issue, while you generally observe Parkerites patting each other on the back and mutually reinforcing the genius of having stocked up on 100-pointers. There are myriad studies suggesting the huge impact of power of suggestion when it comes to wine (reds mistaken for whites and vice versa in one famous study), and it is clear that the palate can be trained and conditioned if one chooses to do so. And yes, there is ego-and insecurity-driven behavior everywhere in the wine world (your Burgundy showing poorly above, but also Parker and the other 1997 Harlan VA-deniers, eh?), but I find that experienced, knowledgable wine drinkers are far more likely to throw in the towel on a flawed bottle and move on than a newbie who has just heard others denigate what was the most expensive bottle in his or her cellar. Again, broad experience teaches one to identify closed or flawed wines and to learn from the disappointing experience.

Also, while I surely have not emphasized it here, clearly neither Parker nor any other reviewer gets it wrong all of the time, and it is true that there are some good modernist wines and lame traditional wines. The truly experienced will tend to give all wines an even shake, and are unlikely to champion traditionalism for its own sake, regardless of how the wines actually taste. There are no doubt some individuals who will mouth one-dimensional, reactionary and unsupportable traditionalist views. however, there is no loud-mouthed, self-righteous, self-validating traditionalist movement (well, maybe excepting Alice Feiring!) to compare to what has gone on in the past three decades with the above-named reviewers, consultants and importers, and no sweeping conversion of modernist fruit bombs back to traditionally made wines (although hopefully that is on its way as the pendulum swings back in many wine regions), and no significant and tragic damage done to the modernist style, so I will have to reject any notion that the traditionalist and modernist camps are but equal and opposite sides of the same coin, with all of the same strengths and shortcomings. Available evidence will not even remotely support that. The wine styles, and the wines themselves, can, however, meet in the middle and achieve some element of balance and harmony. It just has not happened yet, and I think that is due to the extremity of the formulaic modernist style.

Bill, I don’t disagree at all and I believe I understand your position. I would agree that the two sides are not equal even if the underlying psychology can be the same. To a tangible extent Parker enabled a great many to exercise a sense of superiority over traditionalist wine drinkers. In their mind, their wine was “objectively” scored better, how can you tell them they don’t “understand” wine? This upheaval was no doubt spurred on by Parker and the modernist wine that was easy to understand becomes either anti-establishment or a new form of elitism. Unfortunately both attitudes betrays the true nature of wine itself and does no favors to either styles in the end.

My hope and belief is that the passion and art of wine making will supersede the marketing and selling of wine. Stephan Asseo of L’Aventure recalled being told not to forget that wine is “bottled pleasure”. This is true but at the same time I hope that in the traditionalist way we dont forget that each wine is also a “story” as well.

As an aside, I hope that some day it is feasible to do away with the one-sip, “drive-by” tasting of wine. Its a preposterous notion. The idea that anyone, with any type of palate, can determine the value of a wine in a single taste is just insanity.

Exactly. I do like them and I dare anybody, fan or no, to taste a lineup of those Pinots and not see the clear differences from vineyard to vineyard. It’s big modern wine but balanced. Place is muddled when a wine of any size becomes unbalanced… a Cali Cab with made with pruney overripe grapes is just as obliterative of terroir as a vegetal Bordeaux or an extremely acidic Burgundy (and I’ve had several exemplars of all three in the past year). Your mind is drawn to the structural flaw(s) instead of being free to look past the structure into the nature of the wine itself.

posts.name: Taylor Broussard
posts.cooked: There is no doubt that the new world has a sense of place. Paso Robles has a distinct baking spice terroir in a number of varietals. It can be particularly prominent at times in certain vineyards and it has a striking and thoroughly seductive character. (very feminine!) I know with certainty that it is not some type of oak treatment either - I’ve tasted it in both oaked and unoaked wines from a number of different producers across multiple vintages. It’s one of the things I love and look for in many of the wines.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: We discussed Bordeaux terroir earlier, and probably, a few of us used Bordeaux’s relative absence of terroir to flog a bit. While I think that is fair enough, I doubt that the absence of much demonstrable terroir affects the enjoyment of Bordeaux by most people. As I think Ed pointed out above, tasty wines can be made from suspect dirt (otherwise we would have almost no Champagne!), and dreadful wines can be made from the world’s most hallowed ground. I could, however, make a far more convincing case for terroir in almost any place other than Bordeaux, especially California (even if the exploration of that terroir is in its infancy in relative terms), and I think that, where terroir is important and understood, explored and respected, and the vintage cooperates, the best wines on earth result, at every price point. That may account for the fact that at least some of the first-growths are the best wines made in Bordeaux, and have been for centuries in some cases. I believe that Haut-Brion has important terroir, to name one. I think that the overwhelming majority of Bordeaux have nothing but winemaking going for them, and too often, not even that. I understand that some can get pretty effete and smug about the concept of terroir, and why that annoys those who are terroir deniers or just do not give a damn. However, seems to be that there must be some explanation for why DRC RC fetches $10,000+ a bottle and Latour $1,000+ a bottle, and it is not explained by either manipulative winemaking or superior marketing…

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Last night, my old wine buddies Albert and Alfons came in and we opened a bottle of 2011 Hartmann Weissburgunder, an excellent wine from the Ober-Mosel, 4,67 Euro, and a bottle of 1988 Haut-Bailly, no clue what is costs today.

This fantastic wine from Pessac-Léognan is a textbook example of classic Bordeaux. It offers, except of green notes, not only all the notes Marc has mentioned in his post above, but also super delicious fruit. This wine is an unbelievable elegant and balanced wine in all respects, the almost perfect triad of nose, palate, and finish. Such a wine makes an immediate impact on the deeper layers of the psyche, and makes the soul resonant. And for me, this is the most distinguished feature that a wine can have.

No question, the excellent 2004 Pape Clément as well as the 2005 Pape Clément we had the other week offer more fruit and power, but much less soul. That is the experience of three independent, experienced old wine lovers from Saarland where I don’t know any Bordeaux lover who is interested in Parker points. BTW, the 2004 Pape Clément is better than the 2005.

Unfortunately, Parkers demagogical, populist rhetoric - crap as ‘pseudo-intellectuals’ and ‘Eurocentric elitists’- has contaminated the important discussion about traditional vs. modern wine for a long time and continues to do it…

I drank a lot of the 00 Pavie. I don’t think the wine is that modern and it is still quite youthful. As in any other regions, if the terroir is great, it will more than likely outlive the wine making style.

There are perhaps ten to twenty also of the top end “modern” wines which their ability to age is in question. IMO, most of the classified growth from the 09 and the 10 will age well. The true issue with the modern style of wine making is relating to the wines for lalande de pomerol, cotes de blaye, Côtes de Castillon, Fronsac, Canon-Fronsac and etc where the terroirs do not support the modern style of wine making. These wines tend to become hollow and dry as the fruit subsides.

The 1998 which was made with Alain Raynaud and for some reason the 2006. The 2000 is a mess, and I tasted it blind last month, and it is still a mess. It could have been grown in Nicaragua for all the terroir it shows. Sweet, massive, big attack with “gobs of dark fruit” and the subtlety of a kick in the balls. I am absolutely positive that this is not going to show terroir in ten years; it may soften and be easier to drink, but that is about it. I think the lavish hospitality may have colored your view.

Pavie BTW boasts some of the best terroir in St. Emilion. If ever you have the chance to taste the 1964 or 1961, you will see what this estate is capable of.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:

Nick, this may come as a shock to you, but Bordeaux, as the most popular wine in the world (at least it was; I am not sure if that has changed), is what I will call a lowest-common-denominator product…blended to a formula, easy to understand, produced in huge quantities, embraceable by those with undeveloped palates and often little real interest in the larger world of fine wines. Coincidence that, until recently, British food was among the worst on earth and Great Britain was Bordeaux’s number-one market for most of its history? No. .

Been enjoying Bill’s contributions to this thread (as always), but I’m going to call this out as an example of a certain kind of drift that happens among those who question modern Bordeaux – going from expressing doubt about particular examples of modern Bordeaux to questioning the quality of Bordeaux in general. Then there is the similar drift from observing how the terroir concept in Bordeaux differs from the terroir concept in Burgundy to questioning the validity of Bordeaux in general.

Bordeaux has been generally considered to be one of the top if not the very finest of wine regions in the world for centuries, by the most sophisticated connisseurs as well as the general public. I have news for you, folks: all those centuries of experts and consumers are right. If there’s something in your wine-related conceptual apparatus that makes you not understand the ability of Bordeaux to regularly produce some of the finest wines in the world, then the problem is not with Bordeaux, the problem is with you. Bordeaux tends to show up some of the elements of wine criticism that are over-intellectual or based purely on the desire for one-upsmanship or demonstrations of ideological purity, as opposed to coming up with a coherent take on what’s in the glass.

The truth is that Bordeaux has always been a relentlessly commercial and opportunistic region, that sought out and developed its own terroir when better wines were not being produced close enough to the port of Bordeaux for the tastes of local landowners, a place where shrewd merchants mix grapes and plots and locations as needed to make a great product. That may not be very romantic, but guess what? It worked. They produced great wine, indeed they created the model for a certain type of great wine.

As far as terroir goes, to me the questioning of Bordeaux terroir shows up the intellectual incoherence of the notion of ‘terroir’ as frequently used by wine afficianados. Either terroir in the sense of a particular small patch of dirt is essential to making great wine (as opposed to just being essential to permitting the wine expert to wax eloquent about how this wine differs so much from that wine which was made just a few hundred yards away), or it’s not. If it’s not, then what are we arguing about? If it is, then why wouldn’t you think that a good producer in Bordeaux, like a good producer anywhere else, would identify the particular patches of dirt that worked best for them, give it a special and consistent place in their blend, and hold on to that dirt as key to their brand and their product? Just because Bordeaux focuses on the brand is no reason to think that they wouldn’t use ‘terroir’ as a key input to quality.

For myself, I think terroir is much broader than a particular patch of dirt – it encompasses the collision of soil type, drainage, and weather in a particular region, and how the production styles and grape choices have been optimized to that. It seems to me that what’s special about Bordeaux is that it is one of the few non-Mediterranean regions on the planet that can do a good job with ‘heavy’ red grapes like cabernet and merlot (at less than 100 miles north of Provence I would say that the Northern Rhone region still has a heavy Mediterranean climactic influence, although they are admittedly transitional). That is related not simply with climate but with the left bank ‘gravel mounds’ that give such good soil drainage in a relatively humid area. (So there is a role for dirt after all). This is what gives Bordeaux so many of its unique characteristics – the sharp vintage variation, and the way that great Bordeaux can balance both the herbal/soil and the fruity aspects of cabernet.

Also, speaking of Cabernet and Merlot, many of those who criticize Bordeaux will then in the next breath say that they don’t particular like Cab or Merlot. Just one more question then – if that’s true, why should those of us who do give credit to your views on them, any more than a Burgundy fan would be well advised to take advice from someone who doesn’t like Pinot?

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Also, an addition to my previous post that goes to the heart of the matter, for me anyway – Bordeaux may or may not have ‘terroir’, but it definitely has ‘typicite’. There is a readily identifiable Bordeaux flavor profile that I have not found anywhere else, and I can only assume it cannot be produced anywhere else since many have tried. Like all wine regions, that typicity is the product of tradition, soil, and climate, and like all wine regions, it can be lost if winemaking practices change. That is the danger of ‘Parkerization’ to me. I still remember the first Bordeaux I ever had, I believe it was a Chateau Greysac, several decades ago now. That characteristic flavor said ‘France!’ to me and it still does. Although the Bordeaux I drink now are considerably ‘fancier’ than Chateau Greysac, the ones I like best still have something that links them back to that first taste and to all the tastes in between.

Either terroir in the sense of a particular small patch of dirt is essential to making great wine (as opposed to just being essential to permitting the wine expert to wax eloquent about how this wine differs so much from that wine which was made just a few hundred yards away), or it’s not. posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:
Marcus Stanley:

… As far as terroir goes, to me the questioning of Bordeaux terroir shows up the intellectual incoherence of the notion of ‘terroir’ as frequently used by wine afficianados. Either terroir in the sense of a particular small patch of dirt is essential to making great wine (as opposed to just being essential to permitting the wine expert to wax eloquent about how this wine differs so much from that wine which was made just a few hundred yards away), or it’s not. If it’s not, then what are we arguing about?..

To answer your question, Marcus, terroir in the sense of a particular small patch of dirt is a myth, nothing other than a myth. It is only a marketing gag…

Not sure what you mean by this, but there can be soil differences even within a certain area that could give rise to what we call “terroir”. As discussed in another thread (now moved off of Page 1), there is increasing evidence now that microbial communities in the soil interact in a particular way with the nutrients and minerals within that “patch of soil”, giving rise to so-called emergent properties that might be labelled as “terroir”. Predicted by wine makers in the past (see comments by Anselme Selosse), the evidence for these spatial distributions of microbial community and function are being found now in studies of carbon cycling, for example.

Also, speaking of Cabernet and Merlot, many of those who criticize Bordeaux will then in the next breath say that they don’t particular like Cab or Merlot. Just one more question then – if that’s true, why should those of us who do give credit to your views on them, any more than a Burgundy fan would be well advised to take advice from someone who doesn’t like Pinot?

Marcus, well done. The quoted material apparently suggests to you that I do not believe that there has ever been a great Bordeaux. Fair enough, but not so. I have drunk too many great Bordeaux to believe that. However, what I should have done is attempted to distinguish those Bordeaux capable of sustained brilliance, which is a rather smaller percentage of the total, and shot my arrows at the rest. Bordeaux is not unlike any other wine region. Parker himself has observed many times that the wines that he praises in Bordeaux and California are the cream of the crop, and that there are oceans of plonk out there otherwise. Still, because relatively few people alive today have drunk wines from Bordeaux’s long and illustrious history, now fundamentally altered by the modernism, and because only a small percentage of Bordeaux drinkers regularly drink the best from any era, along with the fact of the vast quantities of Bordeaux produced (much of which finds its way to supermarket shelves in France), I am disinclined to give up my “lowest common denominator” assertion. Extraordinary quality has always existed side by side with extraordinary mediocrity in Bordeaux, and for me, the modernists have worsened, not eased, the mediocrity. I believe in the greatness of certain chateaux and their wines more than I believe in the greatness of Bordeaux as a wine region, regardless of its popularity. I am not anti-Cabernet or Merlot per se. However, I do not consider them to be the world’s best varieties, especially Merlot, which, outside of its stellar history at the best Bordeaux addresses and perhaps a handful of excellent expressions in Italy, produces mostly mediocre wine elsewhere and was all but outgrown in California. I am on board with your “typicite” rather than terroir distinction, but as you yourself note, that is at least in major part a product of blending to achieve a certain result. Great Bordeaux can be both a great food wine and a wine of contemplation; Cabernet and Merlot elsewhere are rarely either. Maybe the same case can be made for other regions and grapes as well, but just saying…

However, that is a far cry from letting the grapes continue to hang on the vine as a matter of principle for the sole purpose of creating jammy, high-alcohol, early-drinking wines…

The wine glass on the right is the '61 Pavie and on the left is the last remnant of a '47 Haut Bailly:

I wish I would have photographed the label disclosure on alcohol - my recollection is 12.5%. While that may not be truth in advertising, this wine exhibited no heat or overt extraction.

Ed Murray:
Mark Golodetz:
Robert Alfert:

I had the '61 Pavie at Bern’s a few weeks ago. A wonderful wine, still youthful, crisp. I admitted above to liking some recent Pavie vintages like '01 and 04, but these wines are entirely different stylistically than pre-Perse, and candidly, do not hold a candle to what I had at Bern’s. Been a few years since I had the 2000, so really cannot comment on it right now, other than recalling it was quite modern and appealed to my Napa friends.

Robert,
I think that is the point. There is no chance that Perse’s current wines will ever evolve into something as complex and interesting as the 1961. There will be people who love the wine, even the mature version, but the terroir will forever be missing. Interestingly, the 1998 is actually a throwback to those wines from the 1960s.

With all due respect, without a crystal ball, this is opinion not fact. (The bolded stuff, the bolding is mine.)

Of course it’s opinion. I will say that I highly respect Mark’s knowledge. He may not know it but despite the fact that we like different wines, I carefully read his posts as I consider them grounded. In fact though some may think “modernists” are myopic, I can say that personally I look at posters like Mark’s posts as an opportunity to consider that “other” point-of-view.

But what was highlighted is just opinion and involves a good deal of speculation. When anyone says “no chance” about anything that has yet to evolve they are speculating. I would also guess that even Mark realizes that saying they “won’t be able to give away” the high-scoring modern wines in 20 + years is hyperbole. But more importantly judging a chacteristic like “interesting” just requires more of one’s own subjective filters. I’d also say that when someone says a wine is “interesting” it is often like saying that someone’s wife or girlfriend has a good personality. It’s of value, but also means she is just not attractive:) Ok, I kid.

But seriously, it comes down to the fact that people have different positions in the taste vs (or in realation to) soul or interestingness. Obviously I am in the taste camp. Obviously I like newer Pavie. Honestly I have never had a pre-70s Pavie, but I’ve had a dozen examples from the late 70s to the late 90s. That includes the 90, which was excellent, and the 86 was very good. The other ten or so were just not good wines. Not compared to 98 & beyond Pavie (which admittedly involves my bias), but also not compared to many other St. Emilions of the same vintage/era. The 95 to me was a big disappointment when considering the wine and what I expected from a decent vintage. If one hates modern Pavie, one probably would prefer something like the 91,92,93,94 wines, but the overwhelming number of bordeaux drinkers would take the 98 and beyond wines.

I think a large component of the opinion difference relates to terroir. No surprise, I am not one that has to have terroir, especially in Bordeaux. It is merely a contributor to the taste. I certainly think terroir is more important in Burgundy, in part because the variety expresses it more distinctively. I love Champagne and California cab, and drink a lot of each. Clearly those wines can reach heights in both quality and expense, yet they are just not that expressive of terroir. I would concede the point that ripeness CAN (not always) compete with what people think of terroir. Even I would have a harder time identifying something like the 98/2000 Pavie as compared to almost any vintage of Haut-Brion. That’s an intellectual pursuit. But personally that ease of identification really adds little to the taste and I’d clearly choose the Pavie over mediocre vintage Haut Brion.

I can certainly appreciate the intellectual side of wine. But it is just a part of the equation. For some it can be much more than for others. I think it’s great of someone can rest on volumes of information that they’ve learned from books and tastings to add to their appreciation. But I also think it is silly for someone to suggest as Rudi has that one needs some amount of study to be a “bordeaux lover”. In the end wine is just a luxury consumable product, not unlike truffles, flannery beef, or caviar. Someone should have a basic understanding about wine, but does not need to know the history of St.Emilion, the soil type at Pavie as opposed to L’Evangile, or the date when the grapes were picked to take a sip of 2000 Pavie and decide that they really like or love it.

I do think that Mark and others have touched upon an aging issue. Very true that we will have to see how the wines age. It would be an issue to even those that like them young, if they completely fall apart at 20. But in reality the overwhelming majority of classified Bordeaux does not reach 25 years old. A ton of it is consumed within a dozen years of vintage. even if that is not one’s prefernece, one must realize that it is the way the larger market behaves. Beyond that, different people look for different things in aged wines. It’s just natural that someone who dislikes a wine at realease is not going to love it in 20 years. Personally, I’ve had 98 Pavie 20 times and 2000 Pavie perhaps only 4 times. I think both are definitely improving. We’re 15 years past vinatge on the 98 and it certainly is not falling apart. Noel commented that it isn’t elegant but A) it is not toward the elegant end of the spectrum and B) it will continue to develop. Agreed though, that these wines are not going to develop the real nuances at 20+ years that some other wines will. But the opposite, that they are going to fail to develop at all and fall apart, is really speculation.

I would also agree with Kevin that while 2000 Pavie is modern, it is definitely less ripe, less massive, and more structured than 2005. So in that respect they are different.

If any of these wines were over 13%, I would be astounded.

posts.name: David lo$n
posts.cooked:
I have only been a wine lover for ten years and I am not representing these big wines in st emilion but I do want to give a perspective.

One of the best bordeauxs I ever tasted was a 2001 latour around 2006. It was complex and had beautiful dimensions. Class in a glass. Around that same time I also bought a 2000 latour and a 1996 latour as they were both supposedly much better and they were big but monolithic.

I think everyone here would give layour the benefit of the doubt for the 2000 and 1996 to age and gain complexity. Not saying pavie’s will but no one knows what this stuff will do until given it’s fair time or they blow up and fall apart. Even the reviews of 1990 troplong mondot call for more time.

David

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Unfortunately, the vague term ‘terroir’ is all too often terribly misused. ALL Bordeaux appellations are potentially capable to produce great wines, even the greatest wines, and not only prestigious appellations like Pauillac or Pomerol. Wines like Roc de Cambes, Domaine de Cambes, from Côtes de Bourg or Chateau Martet, Reserve de Famille from Saint-Foy-Bordeaux are every bit as excellent as most of the finest wines from St.-Emilion.
Please don’t forget that more then hundred years ago, the wines from Fronsac were better and more expensive than the wines from Pomerol. Not a particular small patch of dirt, but the skills of a winemaker, his philosophy, and his finances decide on success or failure.
To give yet another example, Clos Vougeot. Whatever the definition of ‘terroir’ could be, one can hardly argue that very different ‘terroirs’ exist inside of the wall-enclosed Grand Cru Clos Vougeot. The vineyard is split among more than 80 owners today, in my youth there were among 70. The quality of Clos Vougeot ranges from breathtaking to very, very modest. Evidently, not the ‘terroir’ makes the wines, the winemakers make the wines.

johngonzales wrote:
… I can certainly appreciate the intellectual side of wine. But it is just a part of the equation. For some it can be much more than for others. I think it’s great of someone can rest on volumes of information that they’ve learned from books and tastings to add to their appreciation. But I also think it is silly for someone to suggest as Rudi has that one needs some amount of study to be a “bordeaux lover”…

John, I didn’t say, “It is a long, long way to become a Bordeaux lover’. I have said, ‘It is a long, long way to become an experienced Bordeaux lover or even a connoisseur.’ That’s a big difference!
Everybody who loves Bordeaux is a Bordeaux lover, no question, but true experience or true competence always includes theoretical knowledge.
I assume that all participants here have theoretical knowledge, but on very different levels. Sometimes I’m really amazed at the audacity of some people who inject themselves into the debate with very little knowledge, and only a handful of speculations. But, if a premise is wrong, no matter how eloquent the arguments that follow, the conclusion is wrong.
Now feel free to moan and whine again, ‘what a silly and arrogant…’

posts.name: Ed Murray
posts.cooked:
Kyle Schlachter:

Back to the intended topic of this thread, you’ve got to love the photo of the “high-tech fermentation room” at Pichon Longueville, circa 2000ish!

What’s wrong with an automated fermentation room? Managing the temperature of the vats is a good thing in my opinion. Many of these automated fermentation rooms are designed to minimize or eliminate processes, that could negatively affect the wine. Using gravity rather than pumps, that’s generally a good thing. Reducing oxygen contact is a good thing. These aren’t evil people trying to FU your wine, they’re trying to make it better/more enjoyable.

Not all wine technology is bad:

WineBerserkers – 29 Nov 13

The Economist on wine technology

Just sharing my story with the WB community in case it’s of interest. Thanks very much to all the Berserkers who offered helpful suggestions.

I personally abhor green pepper in my wine, from the article above:
"Further research showed that pruning leaves at the bottom of the vine sharply reduces methoxypyrazine concentration, one reason why Bordeaux reds taste far less “green” than they once did. "

So - is it traditional to leave the vines unpruned towards the bottom? Where does “traditional” end and “modern” begin?

link_counts.title: The Economist on wine technology - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
Rudi Finkler:

Unfortunately, the vague term ‘terroir’ is all too often terribly misused. ALL Bordeaux appellations are potentially capable to produce great wines, even the greatest wines, and not only prestigious appellations like Pauillac or Pomerol. Wines like Roc de Cambes, Domaine de Cambes, from Côtes de Bourg or Chateau Martet, Reserve de Famille from Saint-Foy-Bordeaux are every bit as excellent as most of the finest wines from St.-Emilion.
Please don’t forget that more then hundred years ago, the wines from Fronsac were better and more expensive than the wines from Pomerol. Not a particular small patch of dirt, but the skills of a winemaker, his philosophy, and his finances decide on success or failure.
To give yet another example, Clos Vougeot. Whatever the definition of ‘terroir’ could be, one can hardly argue that very different ‘terroirs’ exist inside of the wall-enclosed Grand Cru Clos Vougeot. The vineyard is split among more than 80 owners today, in my youth there were among 70. The quality of Clos Vougeot ranges from breathtaking to very, very modest. Evidently, not the ‘terroir’ makes the wines, the winemakers make the wines.

To me, this is a good case in point. The parcels of Clos Vougeot lower on the hill, where the drainage is poor, does not seem to be able to make a great wine. All of the really good Clos Vougeot seems to from high on the hill. So in contrast to your conclusion, I would say that Clos Vougeot is a good example where the terroir does make a difference, although we cannot rule out winemaking, that is for sure. There are many examples of Bordeaux estates that have improved their wine, maximizing the potential of the terroir, when new winemaking techniques or skills have been brought to bear.

Otherwise, I agree with you that Bordeaux has plenty of good terroir. That Bill Klapp denies this really shows in my mind that he does not care much for Bordeaux.

Evidently, not the ‘terroir’ makes the wines, the winemakers make the wines.

To me, this is a good case in point. The parcels of Clos Vougeot lower on the hill, where the drainage is poor, does not seem to be able to make a great wine. All of the really good Clos Vougeot seems to from high on the hill. So in contrast to your conclusion, I would say that Clos Vougeot is a good example where the terroir does make a difference, although we cannot rule out winemaking, that is for sure. Certainly Bill can stand up for himself, and likely will, but I don’t see how you draw that conclusion? To me terroir plays a minor role in Bordeaux as compared to Burgundy. Sure St. Julien wines can show some certain characteristics but I’m inclined to believe other factors weigh much more heavily on the quality of the finished product. These include the winemaker and the blend. How can a wine that is a blend express much terrior?

Rudi Finkler:

… Sometimes I’m really amazed at the audacity of some people who inject themselves into the debate with very little knowledge, and only a handful of speculations. But, if a premise is wrong, no matter how eloquent the arguments that follow, the conclusion is wrong.
Now feel free to moan and whine again, ‘what a silly and arrogant…’

Give the “moan and whine” thing a rest Rudi. It’s the kind of condescension that illustrates my point. As does saying that someone would have the “audacity” to inject themselves into a debate with little knowledge. I’m sure that your knowledge and experiences are siginificant, are certainly large determiners in your drinking decisons, and in fact are even interesting discussion points for all of us on the wine board. But in the general discussion of the bordeaux market, what wines are made, and who likes one style vs. another, knowledge and experience are not the biggest drivers. Or at the most they are just components (to varying degrees) of the largest factor that determines the market and preferences, subjective tastes. So it is very likely that many people, even after reading twenty books on bordeaux and having 100 examples of pre-Perse Pavie, will still love 2000 Pavie. There is a sensory aspect to wine that will always make it subjective.

To assume that another person does not reach a similar conclusion as one’s self because the other lacks the knowledge and experience is a slippery slope. Have you noticed that, reagrdless of being traditionalist or modernists, some of the “educated” people in this thread have a different concept of terroir’s expression and importance than you?

There are definitely tendencies and patterns, but with the massive amounts of bordeaux produced and sold the market is very broad. In a market sense which drives the types of wine made, the educated person gets no extra weight. At least not unless they, and those of like mind, follow through with their wallet. I actually believe that the pendulum has swung a bit too much toward the modern. I don’t think we are going to see a massive return, even when Parker retires, but as with all items of taste, things will swing to some extent.

There are tendencies in the market and current taste preferences that have taken the market to where it is. It isn’t because people buy hundreds of millions of bottles of modern Bordeaux without knowing what it tastes like. I will agree that it is relevant to discuss the segment of the market that is driven PURELY by Parker. But IMO people overestimate the number of people that actually buy what they have not tasted, or at least without knowing that someone like Parker’s tastes correlate well to their own. I also agree that it is relevant to question whether the people that buy a wine will be disappointed in the way it ages at 20 years. The wines are clearly lower in acid than some traditional Bords. I contend that a wine like 2000 Pavie is not going to fall apart in 2020, and will in fact be better than it was in 2004. It btw, is not 15% abv as Bill contends. But again, most people really are not going to age wines 20 years and, if they do, a modernist is not going to look for the same flavors and intellectual return in wine at 20 as the traditionalist.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: I do not care much for modern Bordeaux, for sure, Carl, and will admit to some personal bias on this because most of the Bordeaux that I collect, Haut-Brion being perhaps the best example, are those that can make the strongest cases for distinctive terroir. I do not buy them for some notion of terroir, but rather because I like the wines. And yes, giving Bordeaux at its best all its due, I prefer Barolo, Burgundy, Hermitage and Cote-Rotie to Bordeaux generally, but find that the best, mature examples of each are of the same high quality. I am not a total Bordeaux terroir denier, nor do I mean to say that things cannot be done to maximize what the available land and other factors yield. However, “plenty of good terroir” is too strong for my taste, knowing what I do of Bordeaux’s history. “Plenty of blending and winemaking technique”, yes. And some of it extremely interventionist and some of it not, which is the topic being explored here…on occasion!

To me, this is a good case in point. The parcels of Clos Vougeot lower on the hill, where the drainage is poor, does not seem to be able to make a great wine. All of the really good Clos Vougeot seems to from high on the hill. Certainly Bill can stand up for himself, and likely will, but I don’t see how you draw that conclusion? To me terroir plays a minor role in Bordeaux as compared to Burgundy. Sure St. Julien wines can show some certain characteristics but I’m inclined to believe other factors weigh much more heavily on the quality of the finished product. These include the winemaker and the blend. How can a wine that is a blend express much terrior?

I would say that the terroir in certain areas (Pauillac, St. Julien) is not highly variable, so if the good winemakers are there (I think almost all use the same techniques, the same severe selection), then good to great wine is produced. Head slightly west or south (past St. Julien) or north and the wine goes to nothing–this is terroir, not winemaking skill. But saying the terroir is not highly variable is not the same as saying it is not important. Again, try making Bordeaux to the north or east on the Left Bank.

In general (now there could be serious disagreement here), I think the importance of the winemaking inside the facility (as opposed to in the vineyard) is exaggerated somewhat. As the winemaker at Fontodi said, 90% of the effect is out in the vineyard.

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:

I do not care much for modern Bordeaux, for sure, Carl, and will admit to some personal bias on this because most of the Bordeaux that I collect, Haut-Brion being perhaps the best example, are those that can make the strongest cases for distinctive terroir. I do not buy them for some notion of terroir, but rather because I like the wines. And yes, giving Bordeaux at its best all its due, I prefer Barolo, Burgundy, Hermitage and Cote-Rotie to Bordeaux generally, but find that the best, mature examples of each are of the same high quality. I am not a total Bordeaux terroir denier, nor do I mean to say that things cannot be done to maximize what the available land and other factors yield. However, “plenty of good terroir” is too strong for my taste, knowing what I do of Bordeaux’s history. “Plenty of blending and winemaking technique”, yes. And some of it extremely interventionist and some of it not, which is the topic being explored here…on occasion!

I certainly agree with you for the most part on the non-interventionist route, and like you, consider the Haut Brion among the best precisely because it does show the terroir. But if you deny the role of terroir (as the previous poster did), then the wine is controlled only by the winemaking (which presumably means inside the facility, since in the vineyard one is working with the terroir, whether one likes it or not). So the entire premise of non-interventionist wine making is based on the idea of letting the terroir express itself–if you deny terroir, then there is no basis for promoting a non-interventionist strategy.
