Carl Steefel:
Bill Klapp:

I do not care much for modern Bordeaux, for sure, Carl, and will admit to some personal bias on this because most of the Bordeaux that I collect, Haut-Brion being perhaps the best example, are those that can make the strongest cases for distinctive terroir. I do not buy them for some notion of terroir, but rather because I like the wines. And yes, giving Bordeaux at its best all its due, I prefer Barolo, Burgundy, Hermitage and Cote-Rotie to Bordeaux generally, but find that the best, mature examples of each are of the same high quality. I am not a total Bordeaux terroir denier, nor do I mean to say that things cannot be done to maximize what the available land and other factors yield. However, “plenty of good terroir” is too strong for my taste, knowing what I do of Bordeaux’s history. “Plenty of blending and winemaking technique”, yes. And some of it extremely interventionist and some of it not, which is the topic being explored here…on occasion!

I certainly agree with you for the most part on the non-interventionist route, and like you, consider the Haut Brion among the best precisely because it does show the terroir. But if you deny the role of terroir (as the previous poster did), then the wine is controlled only by the winemaking (which presumably means inside the facility, since in the vineyard one is working with the terroir, whether one likes it or not). So the entire premise of non-interventionist wine making is based on the idea of letting the terroir express itself–if you deny terroir, then there is no basis for promoting a non-interventionist strategy.

Carl, your last sentence sounds good, but the logic is faulty. There are a thousand reasons for promoting non-interventionist winemaking regardless of what you believe about terroir. I think that it is about letting the grapes express themselves in all cases. I can be growing grapes in potting soil with Miracle-Gro and daily watering, and I will surely not be making Haut-Brion from them. However, I can make whatever those grapes are destined to become without intervention, or I can spoofulate to death so that you cannot even tell what variety of grape I started with. (In this example, an extreme one, it could be that the spoofed wine tastes better, but I think that my principle is sound.)

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:
johngonzales:
Rudi Finkler:

… Sometimes I’m really amazed at the audacity of some people who inject themselves into the debate with very little knowledge, and only a handful of speculations. But, if a premise is wrong, no matter how eloquent the arguments that follow, the conclusion is wrong.
Now feel free to moan and whine again, ‘what a silly and arrogant…’

Give the “moan and whine” thing a rest Rudi. It’s the kind of condescension that illustrates my point. As does saying that someone would have the “audacity” to inject themselves into a debate with little knowledge. I’m sure that your knowledge and experiences are siginificant, are certainly large determiners in your drinking decisons, and in fact are even interesting discussion points for all of us on the wine board. But in the general discussion of the bordeaux market, what wines are made, and who likes one style vs. another, knowledge and experience are not the biggest drivers. Or at the most they are just components (to varying degrees) of the largest factor that determines the market and preferences, subjective tastes. So it is very likely that many people, even after reading twenty books on bordeaux and having 100 examples of pre-Perse Pavie, will still love 2000 Pavie. There is a sensory aspect to wine that will always make it subjective.

To assume that another person does not reach a similar conclusion as one’s self because the other lacks the knowledge and experience is a slippery slope. Have you noticed that, reagrdless of being traditionalist or modernists, some of the “educated” people in this thread have a different concept of terroir’s expression and importance than you?

There are definitely tendencies and patterns, but with the massive amounts of bordeaux produced and sold the market is very broad. In a market sense which drives the types of wine made, the educated person gets no extra weight. At least not unless they, and those of like mind, follow through with their wallet. I actually believe that the pendulum has swung a bit too much toward the modern. I don’t think we are going to see a massive return, even when Parker retires, but as with all items of taste, things will swing to some extent.

There are tendencies in the market and current taste preferences that have taken the market to where it is. It isn’t because people buy hundreds of millions of bottles of modern Bordeaux without knowing what it tastes like. I will agree that it is relevant to discuss the segment of the market that is driven PURELY by Parker. But IMO people overestimate the number of people that actually buy what they have not tasted, or at least without knowing that someone like Parker’s tastes correlate well to their own. I also agree that it is relevant to question whether the people that buy a wine will be disappointed in the way it ages at 20 years. The wines are clearly lower in acid than some traditional Bords. I contend that a wine like 2000 Pavie is not going to fall apart in 2020, and will in fact be better than it was in 2004. It btw, is not 15% abv as Bill contends. But again, most people really are not going to age wines 20 years and, if they do, a modernist is not going to look for the same flavors and intellectual return in wine at 20 as the traditionalist.

In a strange way, John, I agree with you, the 2000 Pavie is likely to taste better twenty years down the line than it does today. It will become more integrated, less structured and the tannins will soften. What I don’t think will happen is that it will take on complexity, which is for me, the hallmark of great Bordeaux.

That being said, I don’t expect this argument to change twenty years down the line. The Pavie lovers will say that the way it has developed is the new paradigm in St. Emilion, and those who are on the other side will say that their twenty year old Magdelaine reflects the terroir better. The arguments however eloquently put will never change anyone’s taste, and I suspect the mature bottles won’t either.

I think this really gets to the crux of the matter. A Chateau like Lanessan is just not that good (the terroir is not good enough), so what is done there in the way of modernist wine making (or not) is somewhat beside the point. The real question comes down to how estates with great terroir like Pavie and Cos d’Estournel make their wine, and whether it can develop complexity with years in the bottle. I had that question even with the 1998 Pavie–so backward and extracted (although probably not as bad as the 2000), would it ever be able to move past its monolithic stage??

posts.name: Ed Murray
posts.cooked:
Carl Steefel:
Ed Murray:

Certainly Bill can stand up for himself, and likely will, but I don’t see how you draw that conclusion? To me terroir plays a minor role in Bordeaux as compared to Burgundy. Sure St. Julien wines can show some certain characteristics but I’m inclined to believe other factors weigh much more heavily on the quality of the finished product. These include the winemaker and the blend. How can a wine that is a blend express much terrior?

I would say that the terroir in certain areas (Pauillac, St. Julien) is not highly variable, so if the good winemakers are there (I think almost all use the same techniques, the same severe selection), then good to great wine is produced. Head slightly west or south (past St. Julien) or north and the wine goes to nothing–this is terroir, not winemaking skill. But saying the terroir is not highly variable is not the same as saying it is not important. Again, try making Bordeaux to the north or east on the Left Bank.

In general (now there could be serious disagreement here), I think the importance of the winemaking inside the facility (as opposed to in the vineyard) is exaggerated somewhat. As the winemaker at Fontodi said, 90% of the effect is out in the vineyard.

I wasn’t denying the existence of terroir, just saying it plays a minor role. Certainly there’s a difference between Left Bank and Right Bank wines, even St. Julien vs Pauillac etc. but those are rather large areas. I believe the characteristics which demonstrate a certain terroir are easily and often destroyed by the winemaking process. Is adding petit verdot to make a wine look darker improving the expression of terroir? I generally don’t turn to Bordeaux for a terroir driven experience, for that, I look towards Burgundy.

Mark Golodetz:

In a strange way, John, I agree with you, the 2000 Pavie is likely to taste better twenty years down the line than it does today. It will become more integrated, less structured and the tannins will soften. What I don’t think will happen is that it will take on complexity, which is for me, the hallmark of great Bordeaux.

That being said, I don’t expect this argument to change twenty years down the line. The Pavie lovers will say that the way it has developed is the new paradigm in St. Emilion, and those who are on the other side will say that their twenty year old Magdelaine reflects the terroir better. The arguments however eloquently put will never change anyone’s taste, and I suspect the mature bottles won’t either.

I don’t disagree with you Mark, and I said earlier I appreciate your posts and perspective. My point about the Pavie is as you have alluded to. The “Parker darling” Pavies of 2000, 2003, 2005, and 2010 are definitely ripe and even I agree that they are at or beyond the ripe flavors that some do not care for. But they also have a good deal of structure and a lot of young tannin. The tannins will most definitley soften. I think this is evident in 2000 and 2003 already, though the process will continue for another decade. So that is the respect in which I think we both agree that they wine will improve. I don’t disagree that the acid might be low and that in general these wines will not be the hallmarks of elegance at 30+ that some people appreciate most. My continuing point is that even experienced drinkers look for different things and drink wine at different ages. In fact SOME experienced drinkers enjoy both types of wines. Aside from the implications on the market, how is it different for someone to love traditional and modern bordeaux than it is to love traditional bordeaux and many post 94 California Cabs?

posts.name: Kyle Schlachter
posts.cooked:
Ed Murray:
Kyle Schlachter:

Back to the intended topic of this thread, you’ve got to love the photo of the “high-tech fermentation room” at Pichon Longueville, circa 2000ish!

What’s wrong with an automated fermentation room? Managing the temperature of the vats is a good thing in my opinion. Many of these automated fermentation rooms are designed to minimize or eliminate processes, that could negatively affect the wine. Using gravity rather than pumps, that’s generally a good thing. Reducing oxygen contact is a good thing. These aren’t evil people trying to FU your wine, they’re trying to make it better/more enjoyable.

Not all wine technology is bad:
The Economist on wine technology - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

I personally abhor green pepper in my wine, from the article above:
"Further research showed that pruning leaves at the bottom of the vine sharply reduces methoxypyrazine concentration, one reason why Bordeaux reds taste far less “green” than they once did. "

So - is it traditional to leave the vines unpruned towards the bottom? Where does “traditional” end and “modern” begin?

Absolutely nothing. I just think, with how far technology has evolved in the last 10 years, that photo looks like it was taken during an Apollo mission and not in the 21st century.

I don’t really care if a wine is made using 17th century techniques or the most advanced 21st century wizardry as long as the wine is balanced, interesting and the producer is honest with how it was produced.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Carl Steefel:
Rudi Finkler:

Unfortunately, the vague term ‘terroir’ is all too often terribly misused. ALL Bordeaux appellations are potentially capable to produce great wines, even the greatest wines, and not only prestigious appellations like Pauillac or Pomerol. Wines like Roc de Cambes, Domaine de Cambes, from Côtes de Bourg or Chateau Martet, Reserve de Famille from Saint-Foy-Bordeaux are every bit as excellent as most of the finest wines from St.-Emilion.
Please don’t forget that more then hundred years ago, the wines from Fronsac were better and more expensive than the wines from Pomerol. Not a particular small patch of dirt, but the skills of a winemaker, his philosophy, and his finances decide on success or failure.
To give yet another example, Clos Vougeot. Whatever the definition of ‘terroir’ could be, one can hardly argue that very different ‘terroirs’ exist inside of the wall-enclosed Grand Cru Clos Vougeot. The vineyard is split among more than 80 owners today, in my youth there were among 70. The quality of Clos Vougeot ranges from breathtaking to very, very modest. Evidently, not the ‘terroir’ makes the wines, the winemakers make the wines.

To me, this is a good case in point. The parcels of Clos Vougeot lower on the hill, where the drainage is poor, does not seem to be able to make a great wine. All of the really good Clos Vougeot seems to from high on the hill. So in contrast to your conclusion, I would say that Clos Vougeot is a good example where the terroir does make a difference, although we cannot rule out winemaking, that is for sure. There are many examples of Bordeaux estates that have improved their wine, maximizing the potential of the terroir, when new winemaking techniques or skills have been brought to bear.

Otherwise, I agree with you that Bordeaux has plenty of good terroir. That Bill Klapp denies this really shows in my mind that he does not care much for Bordeaux.

Sorry, Carl, ‘seems’ or ‘does not seem’, that don’t impress me. Where is the evidence?
As for Bordeaux I’m with you.

I thought I was clear enough, but…

I was saying that it was my understanding that most of the variation in quality in Clos Vougeot was due to the differences in terroir, in this case, mostly the drainage of the soils. At the bottom of the hill, the drainage is not good (more fines perhaps, but also less slope)–some have even suggested that some of this ground should have been Village level wine.

I could be wrong (the Burg experts could correct if this is not the case), but I don’t know of a top notch Clos Vougeot made at the bottom of the hill. The ones at the top are the better ones (well, at least of those I have had).

Sort of like terroir in Bordeaux–go south of St. Julien and the wine turns to crap. Or go west, and it drops in quality dramatically. If it was just winemaking, then Prats could go off to the west and make a top notch (and undoubtedly expensive) wine out there…

Fortunately, you have said, ‘I could be wrong’…

Nothing, and I mean nothing, that we could offer as “proof” would be convincing. You would put it down to winemaking differences, subjectivity in tasting, high pressure outside, wrong glasses, whatever. Subjectivity is a bitch when you are looking to nail down something objective.

But then, the goal has never been scientific proof. It is experiencing that one part of the vineyard seems to make better and more distinctive wines than another, and then trying to find out why. The only ways to measure that is either tasting, which by definition is subjective, or breaking down the components in wine, which to date has not been an accurate pointer to terroir. If you want to be an area where you can analyze something and end up with a certainty, then wine is probably not for you.

‘+ 1000’

As for the future of Bordeaux I’m as pessimistic as you. Lemmings or not lemmings, a lot of people love modern Bordeaux. Therefore I do not see any motivation for the winemakers to change their direction.

Why do I get the feeling that there were people around when the majority of vines in Bordeaux were replaced by new varieties (Carménière and Malbec had to leave the scene as the new CS, CF and Merlot entered) that sounded just like Rudi and friends? Personally, I don’t think the dismissal of C was other than correct, but I do feel that a characterful and refined grape such as Malbec really should have been allowed to stay.

For right or for wrong, it’s still just personal taste gainst personal taste. One faith against another. Non-debateable. Suitable only for non-openmindedness that doesn’t want to see or embrace change. There’s a coping strategy available: go with the flow and enjoy what you can. Most wines actually do have a good situation for which they perform very well. And even the great Latour 1982 will be awkward in the company of Chimay icecream.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
Stefan Sandstrom:
Rudi Finkler:

‘+ 1000’

As for the future of Bordeaux I’m as pessimistic as you. Lemmings or not lemmings, a lot of people love modern Bordeaux. Therefore I do not see any motivation for the winemakers to change their direction.

For right or for wrong, it’s still just personal taste gainst personal taste. One faith against another. Non-debateable. Suitable only for non-openmindedness that doesn’t want to see or embrace change. There’s a coping strategy available: go with the flow and enjoy what you can. Most wines actually do have a good situation for which they perform very well. And even the great Latour 1982 will be awkward in the company of Chimay icecream.

Stefan, you seem to have missed the small point that the taste of ONE man and a handful of consultants, not a change in grapes, has led to a sea change in Bordeaux style in a very brief time. The warring palates agree upon that. To call the dynamic involved “non-openmindedness” is naive, and to suggest that “everybody loves some bottle sometime”, and that it is somehow open-minded to try and find the right occasion to drink freakishly atypical wines when you can have something better (and for less money in some cases) is admirably non-judgmental, but, alas, a bit silly. The traditionalists are not trying to deny the modernists the right to drink what they like; God knows that the traditionalists are not buying that style, even if you think that they should for some bizarre reason. Instead, their point is that it is a pity that Bordeaux, along with the New World, slavishly style or restyle their wines in order to cash in on the Parker points lottery. Hardly about nothing more than taste preferences…

Welcome to the asylum!

posts.name: Nick Ryan
posts.cooked: Just tried a 2005 Fleur Cardinale the other day. As everyone is aware this wine is a bete noire of the tradionalist set. When young, it certainly was a huge fruit bomb, as are the 2009 and 2010s today. But I’ve had the 2005 several times over the years, and the fruit has gracefully come into balance with the still copious tannin, and something quite gloriously and undeniably Bordeaux has emerged. It’s easily got another 10 years in it minimum, and I’ll continue to claim those who think modernist Bordeaux won’t age will be proven disastrously wrong (as every 2000 Bordeaux I’ve tried has also demonstrated).

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: Speaking of age-worthiness…I have always thought that to be the weakest arrow in the traditionalist quiver. It is one thing for a spoofulated mess to break up, as a number of low-end Aussies of years ago did. However, with enough fruit, acidity and tannin and a semblance of balance, any red wine will age for a time, and even in low-acid wines, the tannins and fruit will carry the wine until the fruit fades. I think that, for most traditionalists, they speak of relative age-worthiness. They shudder at the thought of a 75-year-old 2009 Pavie, but would revel in a well-kept 1900 Margaux. I agree with that perspective. If I do not want the Pavie now, I cannot imagine wanting it at age 75, even if it lasts that long, and I cannot imagine it becoming a legend. I just drank a 1982 Monfortino that, after 31 years, took 30 hours of air to show its stuff, and even then, not its full repertoire. I can understand that most drinkers today do not want a cellar full of 1982 Monfortino-like wines. However, what such wines can display at maturity is what traditionalists live for…

Bouard had made some fine wines over the years. The 1989 Angelus is relatively modern, but has some serious complexity, and his 1998 is a marvel. It was interesting that I tasted verticals of Pavie and Angelus within the same week, and found many Angelus that I enjoyed, and very little from Pavie.

Obviously the terroir at Pavie is far better than Angelus, but Bouard seems to have a slightly softer touch.

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
Pat Burton:

A while back, a lost of recently tasted traditional chateaus was requested from those defending the traditional style. I saw Bill’s response to this request but no other. Rudi, do you have some favorites?

Pat, I have round about 300 favourites from all Appellations. For you, exclusively, a little selection from what I have opened in recent times:

2005 Le Thil Comte Clary, Pessac-Léognan, 13%, delicious. 2006 D’Issan, Margaux, 12,5%, a favourite among my favourites. 2003 Carruades de Lafitte, 12,5%, Pauillac, as outstanding as the 1999 Lafite, 12,5% we had a few months ago. 2001 Martet – Reserve de Famille, 13%, a gift of Gods. 2005 La Gorce, Médoc, 13,5%, a real surprise. 2003 Ferrière, Margaux, 13%, ubelievable elegant. 2006 Charmail, Haut-Médoc,13%, in the meantime one of my favourites among the Crus Bourgeois. 1999 Moulin Saint-George,St.-Emilion, 13%, as always terrific. 2000 La Prade, Cotes de France, 13%, simply delicious. 2009 Langoa Barton, St.-Julien,13%, the best Langoa I ever had…

As you can see, not a singe 14% alcohol wine! And that is an important part of my buying strategy. Not on any account a 14+% wine!

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: And this…much is made of alcohol level, because of the direct correlation between most Parkerized wines and 14+% alcohol. However, there are inevitably going to be hot-weather vintages where it may be impossible to maintain traditional techniques and keep the alcohol level sub-14%. My question is this: can a wine top 14% natural alcohol and still remain truly traditional, or does the higher alcohol level relegate such a wine to “traditionally made but atypical” status? Or can we even generalize about this with any degree of confidence? Could be that some of us can cite traditional blasts from the past that had high alcohol levels. However, the fact that I have not seen modernists point to much of anything but the sui generis and totally freakish 1947 Cheval Blanc (14.7-14.8%) to support their theories has suggested to me that history is not their friend on this point. In fact, the 1947 Cheval Blanc being completely untraditional actually cuts the wrong way for modernists, unless one is naive and ill-informed enough to believe, as Parker does, that one wine, one freak of nature, should be the model and goal for all modern winemaking.

Thus far there has not been much convincing examples from the traditionalists. I also remember a post where someone mentioning his preference for the 01 and the 06 over the 96 and the 00 which are monumental classic Latours and in case with the 96, showing lovely even at this relatively early stage.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
That was my point, Mark. I was trying to say that the alcohol levels were high in 2010 and, unlike 2009, I am not sure that hot weather accounts for that. I was in Europe for all of 2009 and not 2010, but had the impression that 2010 was a cooler year. True or no? I will confess that I did not study either of those vintages because I did not intend to buy, in part because of age and existng stock, and in part because I hear Parker say “vintage of a lifetime” and run the other way.

Kevin, one other point: Parker’s influence has largely devastated California winemaking over the years, but with pockets of resistance and, more recently, growing numbers of winemakers swinging the pendulum back with some vigor. I find the same influence in Bordeaux, but with differences. One type of wine and only a handful of grapes, for instance. Far less vanity winemaking by rich dudes. A tradition measured in centuries, rather than decades (or a single decade in many cases). However, we see consultants swarming over both regions like locusts. I am not convinced that Parker has done as much damage in the Bordeaux top 50 as he has in California and Australia, but I am skeptical enough to think that the perspectives in this thread are worth collecting.

posts.name: Stefan Sandstrom
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:

Nick, I cannot buy your logic. The fact is that most wine drinkers have life cycles. They start drinking the cheap, easy-to-understand stuff, and then graduate to more expensive, more complicated stuff. Back in the day, I chased some Parker points and drank some oaky Cali Chardonnay and Markham Merlot and a bunch of plonk that seemed great to me at the time. I tasted. I learned. I outgrew those wines and Parker. Nobody is looking to have anybody stop drinking modernist wines because we say so. If anything, are trying to keep traditional wines from disappearing, not just by buying them, but also by educating the next generation about the sins of the Parker having been visited upon the lemmings. Meanwhile, many drinking modern wines right now will outgrow it. And many will change their spots when there are no Parker points to chase. You are giving some in that camp too much credit by assuming that they have a taste for certain wines independent of points…

Far from all wine drinkers graduate or evolve. Almost all wine bottles sold are drunk within 24 hours.

I smile, because I remember the younger version of me that in the 80’ies swore by the Gods that Turning Leaf Zinfandel was the height of human wine making
…then they sold out to Gallo…

posts.name: Stefan Sandstrom
posts.cooked:
Bruce G:
Nick Ryan:

instead will have to take solace in simple economics – start buying more traditional Bordeaux, and therefore encourage more Chateaux to cater to your needs.

Is that really how it works, Nick?
Many of the Bordeaux makers that have gone “modern” were selling through their wines easily enough even back when they were making traditional wines (exceptions noted for genres like the St. Em garagistes, a class of wines that didn’t really exist until late, and therefore never had a traditional incarnation).
The economic motivation to change is, in many cases, not so much “to sell wine”, but “to sell wine at a much higher price”. The Bordelaise have always been extremely concerned with the price of their wine v. those of their neighbors; look at how much the landscape is still impacted by the asking price circa 1855.
Points allow for more aggressive pricing. I do not know of any alternative current model that would allow a Bordeaux chateau to jack up its prices so greatly. I guess if a traditionalist chateau came out and said “unless you traditionalist customers start paying us 150% more per bottle we’re going to go the modern route” that might serve, theoretically. But for all sorts of reasons no winery is going to do this.

…more or less exactly what probably happened when Carmeniere went out and CS in… There were more money to be made with the grapes and blends that ppl actually liked and were prepared to pay for. Maybe the work of house wives making ends meet and stubborn husbands wanting to adhere to traditions, what their granddads had done etc.
…and we all know who wins THOSE arguments

posts.name: Stefan Sandstrom
posts.cooked:
TBroussard:

I’m not nearly old enough to have tasted Bordeaux in the 80s or earlier so it would be difficult for me to compare older Bordeaux that I have tasted more recently with more modern expressions of Bordeaux. Reading through this thread makes me wonder if there’s an element of nostalgia at play but at the same time I have to at least entertain the idea that it’s a fully legitimate concern. We have yet to taste many of these modern examples with any significant bottle age and it may prove to be that the substance and character of the wine remains under the ripe qualities it has now. That’s not necessarily an argument in favor of them, but merely an observation that we deal with the unknown.

Regardless I have to think that if that which is traditional is “good” for it’s own sake (in a philosophical sense) and not merely perception of that which is comfortable, then at some point it will be re-discovered again despite the real or perceived machinations of wine critics. Winemaking despite having economic realities is a matter of passion and art. Even now we see young winemakers in California pursuing “terroir” and more balanced wines. Whether this is idle talk or not I can’t say but I genuinely believe that the pendulum may swing back.

Tastes can be cyclical in all arenas. Design, architecture and even wine-making. No doubt many architects in their time decried the contemporary forms in recent history but it would hardly seem fair for them to criticize the passer-by who admires the clean lines and simple forms. In the same way there is likely some good in the modern wines we see today. Like in all things though it’s a matter of balance. So let the pendulum swing and we will see where we are in a few more years.

Thankyou for sharing your sober view-point.
I’m reading through this mixed thread of knowledge and pointless clashes over the things that were not reasoned in by logic and will not go away with logic (or thrashings)

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Fascinating thread. Neal Martin wrote somewhere once that Angelus is “post modern” and like Mark G, I find more heavy handedness in the Pavie, and comparing is interesting in terms of terroir and because both were made "A"s.

Having said that, I’ll take Trotanoy over both.

Realistically for my budget these days I’ll actually buy none of them, but that’s because of the insane pricing.

I recall purchasing Angelus 98 in Washington DC for $65 per bottle. It is an excellent wine.

Stylistically to me what is most important in Bordeaux is freshness and too often the modernists lack it.

Compare Lascombes to Brane Cantenac. Which is modern? Use a freshness meter and you’ll see.

I dislike spoofilated wines.

I know some people hoo and haw for, say, Cos 2009. En primeur I found it extremely well made and quite boring. Montrose 09? Now, that’s Bordeaux. To take but two opposing examples.

The problem with high alcohol is more pronounced in Saint Emilion. Merlot. I also agree with Mark and others who point to Pomerol somehow “managing the degrees” better overall than in Saint Emilion.

Then again you have the great influence of the Moueix who stress freshness in their wines and serve as good models, from smaller estates like Plince up to the very top.

And let’s not forget the brilliance of VCC.

Well this topic is a long and complex one. Good luck to all

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: Welcome, Panos! I thought that you would find your way here eventually. I like the 2009 Cos vs. Montrose thought, as well as the idea of freshness being the driver. It will be interesting to see what others make of that…

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Very nice post, Panos. I adore, though no longer can easily afford, many of the estates you note. I will say, VCC’s second label, La Gravette, turned out a beauty in 2010.

I agree with your premise entirely if by freshness you mean acid and not “fresh” as in “clean”. As for the latter, I love a little stink in my wine. As for the former, I prefer a solid spline of acidity to lift the weight of the fruit. It is these characteristics, that are so prevalent in Chinon, that has lead me back to Chinon these last 5-6 years, dominating my purchases over Bordeaux (well, price and hold period an issue, too).

A classic example of what I want in Bordeaux is Ch. Lanessan 2009 - red fruit dominant and acid. Now granted, Lanessan is a basic Cru, which clearly punches above its weight class, that will never achieve the heights of Montrose or wines of that ilk. But it’s the classic example of a wine that excelled in a ripe vintage like '09, and if the greater estates with better terroir, were more like Lanessan in style, with better quality of their terroir, I’d be a permanently happy man.

Welcome Bordeaux Samurai! So what do you drink, or better yet, recommend in Bordeaux?

Thankyou.

Wife and I feel our wine preference is towards Hard Rock Café mentality: Love all, serve all. Admittedly though, faulty and plain poor wine we do try to avoid. In the 80’ies and 90’ies I was a dye in the wool traditionalist, but I’ve softened. To us, it just means we are better able to embrace the passions and grand adventures waiting for our discovery out there in the very large Wine World.

We buy and drink Bordeaux according to what we find and like. Not having had all the classified chateaux from all vintages (not aspiring to either) we have found that we enjoy any good quality Bordeaux, regardless of style. If pushed, it would be a tough call between Pauillac and Pomerol, and the answer changing depending on which day it was.

posts.name: Stefan Sandstrom
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:
Stefan Sandstrom:
Rudi Finkler:

‘+ 1000’

As for the future of Bordeaux I’m as pessimistic as you. Lemmings or not lemmings, a lot of people love modern Bordeaux. Therefore I do not see any motivation for the winemakers to change their direction.

For right or for wrong, it’s still just personal taste gainst personal taste. One faith against another. Non-debateable. Suitable only for non-openmindedness that doesn’t want to see or embrace change. There’s a coping strategy available: go with the flow and enjoy what you can. Most wines actually do have a good situation for which they perform very well. And even the great Latour 1982 will be awkward in the company of Chimay icecream.

Stefan, you seem to have missed the small point that the taste of ONE man and a handful of consultants, not a change in grapes, has led to a sea change in Bordeaux style in a very brief time. The warring palates agree upon that. To call the dynamic involved “non-openmindedness” is naive, and to suggest that “everybody loves some bottle sometime”, and that it is somehow open-minded to try and find the right occasion to drink freakishly atypical wines when you can have something better (and for less money in some cases) is admirably non-judgmental, but, alas, a bit silly. The traditionalists are not trying to deny the modernists the right to drink what they like; God knows that the traditionalists are not buying that style, even if you think that they should for some bizarre reason. Instead, their point is that it is a pity that Bordeaux, along with the New World, slavishly style or restyle their wines in order to cash in on the Parker points lottery. Hardly about nothing more than taste preferences…

Welcome to the asylum!

An asylum, for sure.

My reference was to the pre-CS-CF-Merlot era of Bordeaux when Carmeniere was the dominant blue grape. For sure a gigantic swing in the expression of Bordeaux terroir. And at that time, surely lamented by some as the end of all great things as they knew it.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos

Robert Alfert:

Very nice post, Panos. I adore, though no longer can easily afford, many of the estates you note. I will say, VCC’s second label, La Gravette, turned out a beauty in 2010.

I agree with your premise entirely if by freshness you mean acid and not “fresh” as in “clean”. As for the latter, I love a little stink in my wine. As for the former, I prefer a solid spline of acidity to lift the weight of the fruit. It is these characteristics, that are so prevalent in Chinon, that has lead me back to Chinon these last 5-6 years, dominating my purchases over Bordeaux (well, price and hold period an issue, too).

A classic example of what I want in Bordeaux is Ch. Lanessan 2009 - red fruit dominant and acid. Now granted, Lanessan is a basic Cru, which clearly punches above its weight class, that will never achieve the heights of Montrose or wines of that ilk. But it’s the classic example of a wine that excelled in a ripe vintage like '09, and if the greater estates with better terroir, were more like Lanessan in style, with better quality of their terroir, I’d be a permanently happy man.

Hi Robert. Indeed by freshness I mean acidity but more to the point what I sense as optimal ripeness, meaning maintenance of seashell like iodine aspects and lift on the finish!
I agree with your feelings about pricing.
Just lucky enough to go taste every year.
It is frustrating though to appreciate some of these über expensive wines because most mortals cannot buy them. For whatever it’s worth, spoke to a negociant in Bordeaux who expects further drops in price for vintages in the pipeline of first growths and super seconds. Of course they were absurdly high to begin with in both 09 and 10…

posts.name: Mark Golodetz

Panos Kakaviatos:

Fascinating thread. Neal Martin wrote somewhere once that Angelus is “post modern” and like Mark G, I find more heavy handedness in the Pavie, and comparing is interesting in terms of terroir and because both were made "A"s.

Having said that, I’ll take Trotanoy over both.

Realistically for my budget these days I’ll actually buy none of them, but that’s because of the insane pricing.

I recall purchasing Angelus 98 in Washington DC for $65 per bottle. It is an excellent wine.

Stylistically to me what is most important in Bordeaux is freshness and too often the modernists lack it.

Compare Lascombes to Brane Cantenac. Which is modern? Use a freshness meter and you’ll see.

I dislike spoofilated wines.

I know some people hoo and haw for, say, Cos 2009. En primeur I found it extremely well made and quite boring. Montrose 09? Now, that’s Bordeaux. To take but two opposing examples.

The problem with high alcohol is more pronounced in Saint Emilion. Merlot. I also agree with Mark and others who point to Pomerol somehow “managing the degrees” better overall than in Saint Emilion.

Then again you have the great influence of the Moueix who stress freshness in their wines and serve as good models, from smaller estates like Plince up to the very top.

And let’s not forget the brilliance of VCC.

Well this topic is a long and complex one. Good luck to all

Panos, you need to buy Corbin. Despite Sotheby’s wine shop buying out most of the old stock, and putting an obscene mark-up on the wines, I can think of few better buys in the $40 range.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Hi Carl,
Count me as a fan of the 1990 Angelus, but the 1989 even more.
If you look at more recent vintages, equations have “changed”, as there is “more of everything” - including alcohol.
Again, I think that this is endemic to Saint Emilion, and the rising degrees of Merlot in general.
Many estates have to “deal” with that and end up “achieving balance” but in another realm sometimes.
The 2000 is also rather big and muscular.
I like it less than the 1998 (or 1995), which has (have) more elegance.
Whatever your taste may be, Angelus has been successful for many palates because it has been more or less able to make consistently fine wine, even in what are considered weaker vintages such as 93 and 94: I recall enjoying both a few years back, although the 94 is likely better. The estate also has lots of Cabernet Franc and uses that more in blends depending on the vintage, to maintain more minty freshness.
As for “post modern”, you should track down Neal and ask him .
I think what he meant is something like what you wrote at the end of your post, if I may paraphrase: making progressive winemaking decisions without falling into the “modern scourge of extraction and ripeness to the point where nuance and complexity are gone”…
My favorite Angelus since Hubert de Bouard took the estate by the horns in 1988 may end up being the 1998 (or 1989), although the 2001 is very good as is the rather broad 2005, which I think is superior to the 2000. And let’s not forget the fresh 2004, as opposed to the bigger 2003.
Whatever your tastes, prices have reached screwy levels, so the wine has entered what the Bordelais have liked to call “the luxury market”…

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: Kevin, that is the second or third time that you have floated that comment, but the notion that there is a contingent of traditionalists or natural-wine types that prefer unripe fruit is nothing but a Parker and canard. Bordeaux had a string of mediocre to dreadful vintages in the 50s, 60s and 70s, largely driven primarily by crappy weather, not winemaking style, punctuated by a small handful of some of the greatest vintages of all time. Of course great wines come from ripe, balanced vintages. There is, however, no argument to be made in favor of overripe, over-extracted wines lurking there, nor any justification for 16.2% alcohol Bordeaux, under the faulty premise that, if the other elements of the wine can somehow be manipulated to create some semblance of balance, then you have the best of all possible worlds. Wines like the 2009 Cos make a mockery of that misguided notion. You cannot look backward to try and find “modern” wines. The vaunted 1947 Cheval Blanc will not get you there. It is one of a kind, and a freak of nature that has not been duplicated since. There were other superb wines made in 1947…Petrus, Lafleur and others. They are simply legendary wines. Not at all modern, not made according to formulaic misinterpretations of the work of Emile Peynaud, not the product of the allegedly superior 21st-century winemaking techniques that Mr. Leve so fervently fetishes…

If you believe it isn’t, you’ve been on the wine boards too long and miscalculate the influence of Bereserkers etc. There are hundreds of millions of bottles produced every year in Bordeaux. Being generous there are 500 significant bordeaux collectors on this board. Being generous again, give them 700 bordeaux bottles on average. (How many do you own Bill?). That’s 350,000 bottles of bordeaux owned. That’s for ALL vintages and is about 1/100 of the production in bordeaux for EVERY year. I realize that a lot of that production is of lesser chateau, but there is still a shitload of wine produced every year. Sticking with the production for all of Bordeaux, over the 30 vintages beginning with 82, there have been more than 8 BILLION bottles produced. That someone would think anything but a minute percentage of those remain in cellars is unbelievable to me.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Bill,
I am not a big fan of high alcohol Bordeaux and I didn’t like the 16.2% wine tasted during the 10 UGC tasting held in DC. I also would not use the 47 CB to support the modernist camp. I agree that we shouldn’t look backward.

I think we all can agree that almost all great wines will have the ideal ripeness and balance. So what are some of the great examples of traditionally made wines? Because thus far, there has not been too many examples to support the cause, e.g. Rudi’s and the Latour postings.

I just don’t think that “modern” wine making has been detrimental to Bordeaux. The modern wines are still in minority.

Bill, someone posted a citing about wine being consumed within a day or a week of purchase. Whether it’s that day/week or within a couple of yearsr really isn’t the point as it would apply to this discussion. It’s that almost all of the wine doesn’t get consumed at the 10 year+ point. So why ask Mark and Stefan for data supporting the articles they cited and “bitch slap”? That one can say the wine is consumed at age five versus day one really is about the same as it applies here.

It is relevant to the thread that most Bordeaux, even at the $25+ price does not reach 15 years. Because all of the positve attributes that someone might say emerge at 15+ never materialize. One can wax poetic about how the wine should be purchased to be barely touched for those fifteen years, and apply that strategy ones self, but it just is not what goes on for the millions of bottles that producers have to make and sell each year. And while you’d like to think it’s just soccer moms and those with more money than sense, I’d say that most of the people on this board consume the majority of the bordeaux they drink at younger than age 15. The hard-core traditionalists on this thread may not, and that is probably a great strategy, but by my guess there are only 10 of you posting. Cost is a part of the equation. At $75+ per bottle, even adjusted for cost-of-living increases, the cost of building an aged bordeaux celler of 500+ bottles of 15 year old bordeaux is quite prohibitive. People are far less wililling to buy 100 bottles per year to lay down at 2000 and beyond prices. Say one is a Figeac lover. It’s now $175 per for the 2010 IIRC. Assuming one bought just 15 bottles each year, and wanted to wait a decade to drink. By the time one reached the ten-year mark of having their first vintage ready to drink they’d be out $26,000 in Figeac. So aside from the taste preference, using the cellaring strategy requires a different financial capability and dedication. If the Bordelaise want to command so much more money for their wine and sell it in each and every vintage, they have to combat the financial barriers to their customers adopting a cellaring strategy. The most obvious method, aside from lowering price is to allow an adequate group of buyers to not wait as long for the wine to reach peak.
This does not make it the most desirable course for the older drinkers or anyone, but that’s the reality. Even if Parker disappears and tastes equilibrate to valuing wines that age gracefully, the financial barrier remains. I think the “modernized” style inasmuch as the winemaking homogenizes the wines, enables the chateau to have less of a variance from one vintage to the next. They can’t beat a horrible vintage, and they may actually inhibit a great vinatge slightly, but they are able to avoid the fallow vintages where buyers skip and sales are tough. Again perhaps not ideal for a patient consumer, but the reality of the market and why style has shifted.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Well, I think traditional vs modern is a bit misleading. Let’s talk about the fact that Michel Rolland takes credit for calling for riper picking. How about that? But then again, he sometimes went too far for some tastes, including mine. I generally do not like Pavie. Nor do I get too excited about Pape Clement. Although there are vintages of both I like more than others.

I own a bottle of 2000 Pavie and will pour it in another regularly shaped bottle, wrap it aluminum and have it served blind with other 2000s and see how it does. In five years. Who is game? And then I will look foolish for loving it? Perhaps.

All I can say is that - to answer Kevin’s question about naming a fine “traditional wine” it is not really that hard. Calon Segur. Leoville Barton. Figeac. On the Right Bank, Chateau Canon for me is in the “traditional camp” and that goes in quotation marks because even John Kolasa said that the “modernists” have served a useful purpose, if I recall correctly in this article from 2008.

Things evolve, it is called progress.

The nuance is: how far do you go in terms of systematic de-leafing. On both sides. Or how late do you pick?

Especially when you have Merlot and with climate change (in spite of 2013). Here an article I wrote in 2011, where vintners express worry about that.


So “more of everything” to achieve “balance” is not really my cup of tea.

So the question of ideal ripeness and balance is very important but the question of subjective taste can lead to different interpretations of said ideals. As I get older, I like freshness more and more.

In any case, as long as one remains civil and promotes dialogue, then all good.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Hi Folks,

I have only been able to read through about 8 or 9 pages of the posts here, so I may have missed some important points in the discussion. I like Dale’s list from way back in page one pretty well, but have added or subtracted a few estates due to what I think are recent changes at a property since he compiled his listing in 2006. I have placed below the list of who I would call “traditionalists” in Bordeaux these days- not an insignificant number of estates, despite the perception that the region has been pretty much overrun by the modernists in recent times. The thing to remember when scrolling through the list below is that there is no cut and dry demarcation between “traditonalist” and “non-traditionalist” (probably a more useful term than “modernist” for the point of discussion), as the Bordelais are quite reticent about what they actually do in their cellars and it is quite clear that they will do whatever they deem necessary to save a vintage if Mother Nature is seen to conspire against them in a season. There are no Bordeaux estates that I know of that would loss as much of a crop as Chandon de Briailles did for instance in 2008 to stay true to their vision of biodynamics. First of all, most estates are not managed by their owners, so the management team has to answer to the ownership group or person, and secondly, most of these (outside of Pomerol and the tiny St. Emilion properties) are big businesses with a lot of wine to move and IME larger institutions in any business tend to be far more risk averse than smaller enterprises. So probably a lot of the estates on this list would break out the concentrators or the RO machines in a very rainy and thin vintage these days, or resort to other cellar parlor tricks to compensate for what they deem nature did not provide if faced with that or courting disaster for the vintage. And on the list, some estates are certainly more prone to see a threat on the horizon than others and resort to parlor tricks sooner.

So it is not what an estate might or might not do in the cellars in a difficult year that earns them a place (or not) on my list of traditionalists, but rather, whether or not their wines would be deemed classic claret in inspiration to folks such as myself who cut our teeth on vintages of Bordeaux from the 1960s, 1970s and 1980s. For instance, I include Prieure-Lichine on my list of traditionalists, despite Stephane Derenoncourt being the consultant here, as the wine is still done in an elegant, middlewight style that would not strike one as out of character with the property, even if the fine 1983 was the definition of Prieure-Lichine one was using as a benchmark. But, I think that all of these wines would make lovers of traditional claret content to add to their cellars and drink at their apogees of maturity. Naturally, some of these are more “classical” in style than others, as this is a continuum of style and one has to make a cut off somewhere arbitratily to try and construct a campful of traditionalists. Rather than make the list longer than necessary, I have not listed second or third labels of the big estates here, or their satellite properties, but one can usually be pretty confident if the “grand vin” is reasonably traditonal in style, so will the second wine.

Several of the estates on this list make wines that are a bit too new oaky for my own personal palate, as new oak syndrome is still as rampant amongst the classified growths as it is in Vosne-Romanee today, and we are still waiting for the courageous chateau team to step up like Freddy Mugnier did several years ago and define a new paradigm of minimalist new oak for high end wines. For example, the superb wines at Pichon Baron are awfully marked by their new wood these days- so much so that I would not buy them for my own cellar at the present time- but, I still think that they have both feet still at least very close to the traditionalist camp and others who are more tolerant of very new oaky wines would certainly find them fairly traditional in style (beyond the wood). New wood is part of the “luxury goods” wine world these days, and most classified growths in Bordeaux are either already firmly ensconsed in this camp or have aspirations in this direction, so the percentage of new oak in Bordeaux cannot really be a barometer of in which camp the wine belongs.

For me, the things that I look for when trying to ascertain if a property would make it into the traditionalist camp include date of harvesting (is late harvesting the norm here?), malolactic fermentation in barrel, heavy-handed extraction, micro-oxygenation to manipulate palate opulence and tannin perception, and which tonneliers are used (if Taransaud is the tonnelier of choice, it is usually a pretty safe bet that the wine will lean away from the traditionalist side of the ledger- though not always, as Ducru-Beaucaillou is making one of the most classically old school wines in the entire Left Bank these days and yet use exclusively Taransaud for their barrels). And again, there is slippage on some of these fronts with each passing vintage- for example the Moueix properties on the Right Bank, Canon and Beychevelle (another wonderful old school estate) are now using partial malo in barrel for their wines (probably to make them show better earlier for the En Primeur tastings). So there are no hard and fast rules, but these are good parameters to look at along with tasting the wines (when and if possible).

But, the thing to remember is that no one is making as traditional wine today as they did in 1985- there is simply more money now at the estates to buy more new and higher quality oak barrels, be stricter with selection through the use of optical sorting etc, stuff like extraction enzymes and the like were only in their infancy back in the early '80s, and there was generally a different generation in charge of the properties back then- even where the estates remain in the same familial hands, there is not the same generational connnection to the style of claret that was preeminent in the early post-war decades. So one is not going to find the '82 and '09 Calon Segur cut precisely from the same cloth (though one also has to account for climate change and the dramatic differences in the style of the two vintages that one gent compared earlier- try the 2008 Calon-Segur and see if that strikes you as more attune with the style of the brilliant 1982, as 2008 is the second coming of the 1985 vintage at an even higher level of potential quality, whereas 2009 is a torrid vintage in the era of global warming). What can be seen as an uncompromising pursuit of the highest quality possible is also a classic management fear of sparing expense on the latest gadget, gizmo or prevailing winemaking tool and being seen as negligent in their duties and being removed by the financial interests of the estate. So when Leoville Las Cases started routinely using concentrators in the mid-1980s (or did they start with the 1982?) and garnered huge scores for their wines, this correlation was not lost on other neighboring estates and pretty soon everyone had a concentrator or a RO machine or what not. Not that they are always used, but not having the technology on hand if it were necessary could be deemed negligent. Managing a classed growth is a very lucratice gig and no one wants to lose it by not keeping up with the technological advances that are making waves for neighbors. The same dynamic is one of the important impetuses for hiring on one of the trendies consultants.

This post is already too long, and I have not even put up the list, but I wanted to take a quick moment to comment on a few estates I left off of the list. Haut-Brion and LMHB are not here- which would have seemed an insane oversight only a few years ago- but, I sense a very strong push away from the traditionalist camp at both properties in the last several vintages and IMO, the Prince of Luxembourg has pushed on to what he deems as the warmer climes of the modernist school. Maybe I will be proven wrong when the next great, classic vintage like 2008 comes along and both of these wines return to their great historic styles, but the last several vintages have been over the top wannabes to my palate. Likewise, I have not put Margaux, Mouton or Leoville Las Cases on the traditionalist list, as all three (for varying reasons) seem to me to not have their roots any longer in the traditionalist camp- certainly not the same way that Montrose, Latour or Ducru do. Ausone is also missing from the list- though it is by a very, very wide margin my favorite modernist Bordeaux, as there is simply too much terroir at this particular magic spot of earth to keep down with even the slickest, “cuvee de luxe” techniques in the cellar and more than enough new oak to make Bartolo Mascarello roll over in his grave. Anyway, the list, for what its worth, appears below.

All the Best,

John

Thanks so much for taking the time to provide your perspective,John…and certainly not,to my mind,overlong,overbearing and thankfully…not overoaked.

One question:

Collina Rionda Riserva 89 or Petrus 98?

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: Help me, Rionda!

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Bill Bøykin:

Thanks so much for taking the time to provide your perspective,John…and certainly not,to my mind,overlong,overbearing and thankfully…not overoaked.

One question:
One wine to have in your cellar.
One wine only.

Collina Rionda Riserva 89 or Petrus 98?

Hi Bill,

They would make nice bookends btw, but I would take the Collina Rionda without a moment’s hesitation. But, keep in mind, despite the fact we have been discussing in another thread just how good the 1998 Right Bank wines are, the 1989 Piemonte vintage is the 1961 vintage for Bordeaux- a once or twice in a lifetime vintage of seismic proportions and unfathomable potential. There have been an awful lot of superb vintages in the Langhe since 1989, and yet, to my palate, none of these subsequent years has been able to aspire to the summit that 1989 currently sits comfortably atop. So, if you asked me '61 Petrus or '89 Collina Rionda, I might have to first ask to consult a crystal ball to see how long I could be planning for the future, as the '89 Collina Rionda is still really a puppy and I just might have a mind to drink it at its apogee!

All the Best,

John

Excuse me, John, but the 1989 vintage is vying for the SECOND slot of the “twice in a lifetime” vintage competition with 1996 and perhaps 2004, the first being held by 1978. Every time that I open a 1978 Giacosa red label, I realize that, as admittedly great as the 1989 CR is, it is, all in all, another brick in the (Maestro’s) wall. And what a wall!

You showing up here has already paid huge dividends. Within 5 minutes, Mike Steinberger shows up out of nowhere and the two of you engage in a spirited, gentlemanly pissing contest over on the 1998 Right Bank thread. Now, however, with your remarks on recent-vintage H-B and La Mish, you have gone to the very heart of why I started this thread to begin with. I am yet sure what the truth is about those two wines in 2009 and 2010, whether your assessment is right or whether the wines remain traditionally made, are victims of weather patterns and you are merely jumping to contusions (and not for the first time!). It seemed that you left the door open for those two to return to the traditional fold in the future, so perhaps you are not certain, either, at this point. However, I very much appreciate your thoughtful post and traditionalist list. I suspect that 2009 may go down as the make-or-break vintage in this debate. If it is a modern-day 1947, the modernistas and the reviewers who hyped the vintage into the stratosphere will be vindicated. If not, maybe we will not see so much 14.5-16% alcohol Bordeaux in the future. You can never safely generalize across wine regions, of course, but 2009 was a hot, dry, nasty summer in the Piemonte, seeming nearly as bad as 2003, and I have no intention of buying or drinking its wines here. I was thus surprised when the early ballyhoo was heard for both Bordeaux and Burgundy. I did not buy the 2009 vintage in either of those regions, either, as much due to age and good supply as skepticism. For me, the jury is still out, but Burgundy seems to have 2009 bona fides that Bordeaux lacks. It is rather fun to have tasted a few wines, like the 2009 Cos, to understand the diversity of opinion, but also to not have a dog in the hunt for the first time in many years, and to be able to study the 2009 and 2010 vintages slowly and with detachment. I suspect that I will succumb to buying single bottles (more like half-bottles) or tiny quantities of H-B, La Mish, Latour and a couple of others for scientific purposes, but otherwise, I will just sit back, backfilling pristine 1961, 1964, 1967, 1971 and 1978 Nebbiolo whenever I can, and watch this thread evolve!

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Hi Bill,

I am in full accord with you regarding 2009 and 2010 and do not have much hope for a majority of those wines from either vintage in Bordeaux (and not much for the majority of 2009 Burgundies either, but that deserves another thread). Interestingly, I liked a great many more wines in 2009 than in 2010, as I felt that there was the possibility (particularly on the Left Bank) to harness the power of the vintage a bit in '09 and make a ripe, powerful, but well-structured wine that could just end up being pretty special- Latour, Ducru or Montrose are good examples of how good an old school 2009 one could make. The drought of 2010 made things tougher on vignerons on both banks in Bordeaux, as the tannins were lagging so far behind the sugars (which were concentrating to alarming levels due to dehydration of the berries) that one had to leave the grapes hanging even as the potential alcohol levels were moving the settings on their phasers up from stun to kill. It’s a strange year with a lot of strange wines on the Gironde and one hopes that it does not become a more typical type of year as climate change further evolves growing seasons here. The funny thing for me is that the Bordelais have indeed had a great vintage in recent times- the 2008s- and yet, only a few vignerons like to talk about those wines (perhaps for fear of upsetting the delicate balance of financial interests deeply entangled in the 2009s and 2010s for investment funds?) at the present time, but for my palate, the vintage towers above both '09 and '10 and is the last Bordeaux vintage I bought for my own cellar. Of course, my favorite vintage of the 1980s is 1985 (which the 2008s share some resemblance to), so my palate predilections are pretty predictable I suppose.

In the world of Haut-Brion and La Mission I really do not know what is going on here, but we now have four vintages in a row that I really did not like at either property, but I hold out hope that this can be blamed on the weather (though not sure how the aggressive new Taransaud oak spice in these four vintages can be weather-related) and these two flagship estates will not plunge over the precipice for good. But, the sense of drift here is palpable when visiting, if one bothers to pay attention to these types of things. So, it is a very good time to be backfilling Baroli and Barbaresci from the decade of the 1960s and 1970s mon ami and very glad to see you included the very underrated and superb 1967s on your shopping list! BTW, '78 CRR is my reference point for where the 1989 is headed in the fullness of time- have had the wine on a couple of occasions and fully agree that if it is not heaven to be drinking that wine, one can at least see heaven from the summit of the '78 CRR.

All the Best,

John

I spoke with John privately about this. He told me that he adores Pavie, and, beginning with the 2000 vintage, has put away multiple cases of it every vintage. posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Gary York:

Seems Pavie was not on JG’s list, an oversight for sure. And how is high Alc not a marker for mode, sorry, non-traditional Bordeaux?

Hi Gary,

Certainly high alcohol should have been on the list, but I thought it might be covered under “late harvesting”. The thing is that Mother Nature does not always cooperate with the late harvesters, such as in 2008, when it was simply impossible to let the grapes hang much longer than your neighbors’, as nothing ripened until the start of October in any case. It is one of the reasons that I enjoy the vintage so much, as there are a lot of wines that I would studiously avoid in earlier ripening years that I found far more potable than usual in 2008, as the producers’ hands were tied in terms of picking dates.

Re Chateau Pavie, all I can say is that Bill is a wily fox…

All the Best,

John

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Panos Kakaviatos:

John,
One question: why is Leoville Barton not on your list?

Hi Panos,

I have been wondering where Leoville Barton is headed for many years now, and a vertical tasting that Mark G organized of Beychevelle and Barton only further added to my confusion about LB these days. I think you came up for the tasting, if memory serves me correctly. The Barton wines all seemed “forced” stylistically to my palate, with much more overtly new oaky personalities, much more extraction than back in the decade of the 1980s (when I absolutely fell in love with the property and drank tons of the estate’s wines), and a creamy textural element on the palate that made me think that the malo was being done in barrel. I was told by our Barton family member that the estate has never done malo in barrel, so there has to be another tool at play in the cellars (micro-oxygenation perhaps) to give the wine that creamy textural thing that I find so abhorant in Bordeaux. In any case, LB is not the wine it was in the decade of the 1980s, and hence why it did not make my list, though I do know that a lot of other folks like the wines just fine still and find me hyper-sensitive. Maybe I was just too big of a fan of wines like the '85 and '89 to join LB in the modern world of 21st century claret!

All the Best,

John

posts.name: Rudi Finkler
posts.cooked:
The traditionalists are no monolithic block, and there will always be differences of perception, knowledge, taste and opinion. Everyone draws their own demarcation line between good and bad, but traditionalists are most likely to reject today’s monstrosities. This is aggravated by the fact that the prices of many illustrious wines are simply ridiculous.
Over the last decade, I have heard from many traditionalists that they feel offended and declassed. Most of them have stopped buying Bordeaux in the meantime, and I wonder whether that is desirable for the winemakers in Bordeaux?
Of course, in today’s global village there are enough fancy people who can replace traditional groups of buyers, but will they prove to be just as reliable as the traditionalists?

Rudi; Nice post! I agree with your sentiments and would add that many traditionalists , including me, have also advanced to an age where they no longer need to buy wines that need 15-20 years to show their best.
If you combine age with the ridiculous pricing you have reason enough to avoid Bordeaux purchasing .

Cheers!
Marshall

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Bill Klapp:
Gary York:

Seems Pavie was not on JG’s list, an oversight for sure. And how is high Alc not a marker for mode, sorry, non-traditional Bordeaux?

I spoke with John privately about this. He told me that he adores Pavie, and, beginning with the 2000 vintage, has put away multiple cases of it every vintage. However, he is constantly worried that, if he goes public with his high opinion, the prices for subsequent vintages are likely to go through the roof, and the available stocks will disappear. Being the anti-Parker, John is sensitive to such things, and thus, chooses to remain silent on the subject of Pavie…

I have photographs of John tasting the 2009 and 2010 Pavie. I am saving them for when I need retirement money, and I can either blackmail John or sell them to the Enquirer. John knew I would be taking a photo of him tasting the '010, but even so, he was unprepared for its, how can I put it, gutsy personality, so the photo of him in great pain, is absolutely genuine.

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
John Gilman:
Panos Kakaviatos:

John,
One question: why is Leoville Barton not on your list?

Hi Panos,

I have been wondering where Leoville Barton is headed for many years now, and a vertical tasting that Mark G organized of Beychevelle and Barton only further added to my confusion about LB these days. I think you came up for the tasting, if memory serves me correctly. The Barton wines all seemed “forced” stylistically to my palate, with much more overtly new oaky personalities, much more extraction than back in the decade of the 1980s (when I absolutely fell in love with the property and drank tons of the estate’s wines), and a creamy textural element on the palate that made me think that the malo was being done in barrel. I was told by our Barton family member that the estate has never done malo in barrel, so there has to be another tool at play in the cellars (micro-oxygenation perhaps) to give the wine that creamy textural thing that I find so abhorant in Bordeaux. In any case, LB is not the wine it was in the decade of the 1980s, and hence why it did not make my list, though I do know that a lot of other folks like the wines just fine still and find me hyper-sensitive. Maybe I was just too big of a fan of wines like the '85 and '89 to join LB in the modern world of 21st century claret!

All the Best,

John

Which vintage do you see this style arising? We had a vertical tasting of Leoville Barton from 1982 to 2000, and at least through 1996 their house style was recognizable–firm, austere even, and I would say overall traditional. One could plot the years to peak drinking for each vintage and it fell on a straight line with “ready” at 20 years (i.e., the 18 year old was close, but not quite there, the 14 year old less so, and so on).

But with the 2000, it showed as almost impenetrable back then. Later bottles were not much better, with the wine showing essentially as monolithic. Maybe these just need more time–not sure, but I flipped most of mine.

posts.name: Panos Kakaviatos
posts.cooked:
Thanks John and Carl for your reaction to the Leoville Barton question I posed.
Allow me to defend Leoville Barton as being in the traditional camp, and in a positive vein, although I give that the 2010 was quite imposing. And since we are talking about John’s fine list, allow me to critique a bit the other notable LB, being Lynch Bages .

First the Barton.

I had the 2000 Barton and thought was absolutely lovely. Lilian Barton had found it a bit “soft” in the past, but I think it is one of the very best Medocs in its price point from that vintage.
Same for the 2005 and 2009 by the way.
And the 2003 is a darn good wine, considering the torrid nature of that vintage, with 02 and 04 excellent buys, with freshness.

As for Lynch Bages, I feel like that since Sylvie left, the style has become bigger and more modern, and less to my liking - although it can be still very good, with appeal in certain vintages.

But with its prices going nutso, in recent vintages, GPL is my more obvious choice between these two often compared fifths - and GPL is made from earlier harvesting with less extraction of tannin, generally speaking.

Anyway, I will get me more Corbin, as I do have a bottle of the 2009 and while working at the Council of Europe liked it so much that got the protocol division to purchase a case for the cellar of the organisation’s secretary general.

And by the way, this thread more than illustrates the fact that indeed “traditionalists” or those who lean in that direction, and I would put myself in that camp, are “no monolithic block” and that “there will always be differences of perception, knowledge, taste and opinion”. Indeed:

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Lanessan stopped being traditional in 2014? So when people recommend it to me they mean pre-2014?

NO, no, 2014 Lanessan is a classic. If you have access to this wine, buy it. I will be so bold as to say, if you don’t like this wine, you are not into well-made, four-square classic Bordeaux. I bought several cases of this wine in this vintage, like I also did in another winner vintage for Lanessan, 2009. This is the wine you pop on a Tuesday night with some basic fare, you want a classic wine without breaking the bank, and it just hits on all cylinders. It is a perfect 90-pointer on my grading scale. My average price is sub-$20 USD. Not many comparable values in the world of wine.

Sadly, Lanessan brought in Bouard to consult for 2015 and 2016. The style has changed. I’m out.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Dale Williams:

More or Less Traditionals
Vieux Chateau Certan - An amazing, classic Pomerol, worth every penny
Certan de May - I still waffle on this, often the oak presence is more than I like
Leoville-Barton - a classic stalwart
Figeac - Flipped to the dark side, but still excellent in 2014. I bought but did not try the 2015
Trotanoy - Another amazing, classic Pomerol. Daddy needs more of this.
La Louviere - Flipped to the dark, stick to 2005 and earlier
Meyney - Lost its course 2005-2014(?), but I am hearing 2016 is excellent
Haut-Bailly - Pretty classic, only 2009 was a bit over-done for me
Magdelaine - An OMFG St Em, last vintage was 2011. If you see it, grab it and run like you stole it
Latour - Shock and awe, if you can afford it, do it.
Lafite-Rothschild - A gloriously classic, elegant, best of class Bordeaux
Haut-Brion - ditto
Cantemerle - Classic, and amazing QPR.
Lanessan - Went dark in 2015, but 2014 in bulk
GPL - Classic, never blow me away but I always enjoy it, like doubles and triples
Talbot
LMHB - Classic.
L’Arrossee
Cheval Blanc - A spellbinding classic
L’Evangile - Gorgeous, classic Pomerol
Lafleur - One of the very few fine Bordeaux that I have never tried, I’m too poor, evidently.
Sociando - the classic of the classics, quite iconoclastic - i own more of this than any other Bordeaux
Bel Air Marquis d’Aligre - A geek’s wine. What Bordeaux likely tasted like in the 1800s

That’s a solid list, Dale. Some of my favorite Bordeaux and standard purchases right there (well, except the for the pricey first growths), including from the recent 2010 vintage.

Loved that you highlighted La Louviere. That was my epiphany Bordeaux - the 1990 vintage, which I had in '93. Was young, for sure, but just absolutely delicious. I had an intuitive, but very basic understanding of terroir from that wine alone. Your post just reminded me I need to go find more from that estate.

Chris, Jan -

I think Dale’s list from 2013, quoted above, remains pretty solid. I added few thoughts in bold. Bear in mind that my last big foray into buying a vintage broadly was 2014. While I stay on top of Bordeaux, I really cannot speak to the 2018 or 2019 vintage.

Interesting that Dale did not put Montrose on his list. Pricey, and takes like a gazillion years to come around, but I think it is classic. Buy the second label in 2016, La Dame de Montrose.

If you can find some of the Cordier wines from the 1980s, they are worth grabbing, as they represent what is to me, the epiphany of classic, old world - and yes slightly funky - Bordeaux. Most Bordeaux are, according to many, better made now, but these classics still resonate with me. The 82, 86 and 89 vintages rocked.

Gruard Larose
Talbot
Meyney
Cantemerle
La Lagune

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
There are some extremely traditional wines not mentioned on the list above - Batailley, Grand Corbin Despagne, Soutard, Le Gay etc. They age well, but are tough to drink when young. (Le Gay might be different now though)

Before one goes and stocks a cellar in an absolutist way of ‘only one style’, my suggestion is to taste some of these different wines blind, and with some age on them, to see what you really like.

There are also modern styled cru bourgeoise / petit chateaux, and among those, I think St Paul or Charmail are worth trying. Not exactly the same as a Pavie Decesse, but perhaps 1/6 the price for the entry ticket.

One other caution - be careful buying case lots until you know what you like. Circumstances and tastes will change over time, and I would say on balance, the times I’ve thought “gosh I’m glad I have another 11 bottles of this” have been outweighed by the “hmmm, I think I don’t love this anymore and I better use this for spouses quaffing with friends”

The tried and true piece of advice still holds true. Taste, Taste, Taste. Find a group, form a group, just make sure you open a lot of bottles.

Indeed, nothing can replace own experience. There is, however, a simple and effective way to significantly reduce the number of disappointments: If you are looking for traditional red Bordeaux, avoid any wine with more than 13,5% alcohol!

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
I’m pretty well aligned with Dale’s list though as noted above some properties have shifted modern since he wrote it 7 years ago. It would be great if the first post in this thread could be edited to keep an up-to-date list where it’s easy to find, without having to start a new thread and lose the great discussion here. Like Pat Burton’s Italian traditional/modern thread. If the mods can arrange it, I’d nominate Robert to take over maintenance of the OP.

Robert says Figeac went modern after 2012, Lanessan after 2014, Conseillante after 2014, Carmes Haut Brion after 2010/11. I’d add Canon to this group. I don’t follow it every year so can’t say when it changed. I’d put the 2015 in the moderate or mid-modern category.

Haut Brion and La Miss have shifted some but are still closer to traditional than moderate to my palate.

Pichon Lalande varies from traditional to moderate depending on the year. That nice green streak isn’t as prominent as often as it used to be.
