The change in HB and LMHB were noticeable with the 2009/2010 vintages. Both have significant holdings of Merlot, where the effects of later harvests and modern technology seems to have an exaggerated effect.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
Just finished and rather enjoyed the 2017 Lanessan. Here’s my note from CT:

Pop and pour and consumed over 3 days. Sealed under Diam. Quite shy nose, blackberries, cassis and some unobtrusive notes of (old) oak. Palate is light to medium bodied, very fresh with some slightly spiky tannins and tart almost savory fruit, some green pepper on finish. Showed best on day 3: nose has gained focus and precision, with smoke, tobacco, red fruits. Palate has a more complete feel. Traditional and nothing spoofy going on here! 89+ points for today, but I feel this has plenty in the tank and will be best with a few more more years on it

As a data point to revive this old thread, this struck as pretty classic claret in style, nothing glossy going-on palate wise and while initially there was some oak on nose it had none of that vanilla character. Impressed by where this got to over 3 days.

Does this mean the Bouard controversy is limited to the 15 and 16 vintages? Do folks consider those to definitively be outliers to the house style?

As an aside, I recently received delivery of part of my winter firewood. This was all oak, and unfortunately seemed too fresh and not particularly well seasoned. The upside though has been the opportunity to get smell a perfect example of new oak! And yes, some Bordeaux really does indeed smell like a freshly split oak log! Just add a dollop of black current jam and the match would be perfect…

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked:
I have been drinking late 1990s and early 2000s Pontet Canet, and there is nothing spoofy/modern about it. It’s classic Bordeaux. Can’t speak to what might have happened after 2004, as the price escalated beyond my willingness to pay, but until then it’s solid.

A winemaker friend had been on a visit to Pontet Canet and had brought a few bottles back and he was nice enough to open one about 2 years ago. I can’t for the life remember what vintage it was, but certainly after 2016. In my very untrained palate, it felt pretty old world to me. I though it was great.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I find Pontet Canet pretty boring and universal in style. Drink it side-by-side with say a Leoville Barton of the same vintage, and it shows. I think I have cleared out every single bottle of PC from my stash. I would not call it spoofy like a Lascombes, Pape Clement or Troplong, but it definitely shows the modernist consultant bent.

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked: But Robert, aren’t they completely biodynamic and organic these days? And they even farm using only horse and carriage to respect the old ways. Are you saying they employ all these old world things only “front of house” so to speak, and then produce a modern Rollandized wine at the back of the house? It seems a little counterintuitive to go through all that rigmarole in the vineyard to then turn to manipulative winemaking in the cellar.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
But Robert, aren’t they completely biodynamic and organic these days? And they even farm using only horse and carriage to respect the old ways. Are you saying they employ all these old world things only “front of house” so to speak, and then produce a modern Rollandized wine at the back of the house? It seems a little counterintuitive to go through all that rigmarole in the vineyard to then turn to manipulative winemaking in the cellar.

I don’t have a lot of experience with Pontet Canet, although I did visit there in 2014 when I was in Bordeaux. Their approach seemed to be more “new age” rather than traditional or Rolland. For example, they told me that they farm with horses because (1) they are gentler on the soil and do not compact the soil as much as tractors do (makes sense) and (2) because the farm animals communicate with the vines and make the vines happier (I will let you judge that one). They were making or aging wines in various types of vessels including concrete eggs and concrete amphora. So, I have no real idea where the resulting wine shakes out (with time) after all this. My guess is not traditional or Rolland.

I find PC too polished to be on the traditional side, with its slightly sleek and sweet fruit. But I wouldn’t call it modern the way other Rolland wines clearly are. For my taste I’d say indeed a little boring, but that’s very much a personal judgement based on limited arbitrary samples.

Yesterday we drank a Troplong Mondot ‘98. Usually I stay away from this property for obvious reasons, but this was very enjoyable and almost classic St. Emilion. Only 13% stated on the label, which gave a hint. I was impressed.

I’ve had the 09 & 10 Pape Clement (not decanted) as part of larger horizontal tastings and both did disappoint (too much extraction and cheap oak). Opened the 2015 for a vintage tasting and it was in the same horrible category. The two of us tasted it and were certain that this will easily recognizable as the weakest wine in the blind tasting due to all the unintegrated oak. Surprise, surprise after double decanting the wine and letting it rest for 8 hours back in the bottle, the wine didn’t show the oak anymore and was actually really liked (on a 94-96 level) by the group. Still clearly very much in the modern camp.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked:
1. Langoa vs Leoville - Visited the Chateau last summer and tasted both wines side by side. I find Leoville to be darker fruited and Langoa more on the red spectrum, and lighter in density/extraction (although not lacking in tannin). For my palate, Leoville is the far superior wine. I would imagine that Langoa would be accessible sooner but don’t have much experience. As far as no one talking about LB, the 2016 was Spectator’s wine of the year for goodness’ sake!

  2. Re/Pontet - I would submit they underwent additional stylistic change post 2014. Both the 2015/2016 vintages have WAY less extraction than previous vintages. Both kind of glossy in texture, but light in the glass and on the palate and not Rolland-ish really in any sense. Compared to 04/6/8 and they are a different animal with much more finesse. I’m long on 15/16 PC.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
What have you had, Steve? I ask because I haven’t had a large swath of 02 Left Bank, but I have thoroughly enjoyed the 02s I have had. They’re not blockbusters but I’ve found the more middling structure and extract have given them real charm in a friendlier, complex, highly aromatic*, traditional style.

Off the top of my head, these 02s have charmed:
Leoville Barton
Leoville Las Cases
Pichon Lalande
Pichon Baron
d’Issan
Gloria
Sociando Mallet
Domaine de Chevalier
Branaire Ducru
Haut Bailly

*A Bordeaux lover with 50 years of tasting experience has long maintained that broadly speaking there is a trade off in claret between aromatic expressiveness and nuance and palate power and extract. I think there’s much to this theory and the 02 Left Bank are the case in point.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Had a bottle of the 2018 Pichon Comtesse Reserve tonight, and it’s neither tradition nor modern, by the definitions outlined here 10 years ago. Label says 14.5%, but carries it effortlessly. Despite the abundance of fruit (it is 2018 after all), one would be hard pressed to mistake it for Napa - it is left bank Bordeaux through and through, superbly taut and mineral forward.

To me, it exemplifies the new “post-modern” wave of Bordeaux, showcasing full phenolic complexity, but without sacrificing finesse, freshness and regional typicity. I’ve seen this across the board in recent years, from wines formerly considered traditional (Montrose, Canon) and those formerly considered modern (DDC, Troplong Mondot, etc), and everything in between.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The issue is more that lots of plantings in the 80s, 90s and 00s occurred in a context when ripening was challenging, so rootstocks and clones that ripen rapidly were favored. In a very different context of warmer, sunnier vintages, that translates to ripeness being attained only at high alcoholic degrees. Picking unripe is not really the solution. So while you can do a lot in the vineyard and winery to mitigate overripe flavors and create fresh, vibrant wines, the issue of high alcoholic degrees is not such an easy fix. And this is particularly a problem for Merlot, which naturally delivers higher abvs and tends to ripen earlier, anyway.

posts.name: William Kelley

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Btw all this was in my essay published at the beginning of this year. See the first paragraph under “Challenges”:

This picture of the state of the art in contemporary Bordeaux has so far been extremely positive. But what challenges does the region face today? Most obviously, climate change is clearly testing for the region, which witnessed unprecedently hot and dry conditions as recently as last year, accompanied by forest fires of an extent not seen since 1949. While I am convinced that the agronomic adaptations outlined earlier in this essay mean that Bordeaux can adapt, without changing its traditional cépages or reinventing its identity, the reality is that many of the choices of rootstocks and clonal material made in the last 50 years were made with a view to achieving early ripening. This won’t change overnight, and in the meantime, many estates in Bordeaux are likely to continue to struggle with elevated alcohol levels and high pHs in sunny, warm vintages, even if more attention to harvest dates and adaptations in élevage practices (as outlined above) can do much to retain vibrant flavors and avoid overripe characteristics in the finished wines.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Bit of a naive question - but for arguments sake lets say that Bordeaux weather is becoming more like Napa - are the rootstocks and clonal material that are utilized in Napa more suited for Bordeaux?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: One of the reason that ABVs are so high in Napa is that they planted Bordeaux clones

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The problem is not unique to Bordeaux. If you have clone 777 Pinot Noir on 161-49 rootstock in Burgundy in a ripe site, for example, it was difficult to produce a wine with less than 14% alcohol in a vintage such as 2022 if you wanted to attain physiological maturity. Virus free massale selections and less precocious rootstocks are likely the solution, but that entails replanting… so you factor in replacing the trellising (materials and labor) at around let’s say 30,000 EUR per hectare, 50,000 EUR for the new plants, the cost of at least three years out of production and ideally longer… and you have quite an unattractive proposition if your existing middle aged vineyard is producing wines that people are happily buying.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: So it’s the converse - the reason Napa is so modern in taste is Bordeaux’s fault! It is just that the weather has changed and now Bordeaux tastes more like Napa. I guess the question is where do you find clones that ripen slowly and kind of mitigate the effects of hotter weather (since it isn’t from California)?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: A clonal selection takes a long time to come to fruition. But there are new clones coming onto the market from time to time. And then there are so-called “massale” selections, which are really just a mixed bag of clones, say a dozen or so individuals, that have been selected and treated for virus and propagated. Massale selections of heritage vine material, however, permit much more genetic diversity and are generally less likely to have been isolated for narrow technical parameters (e.g. maximum sugar accumulation for maximum yield). Later-ripening massale selections are likely the way forward; but we should also be sure to preserve all manner of genetic material for the future as the key to the durability of our noble varieties is their genetic diversity in my view!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I find that Bordeaux and Napa Cabernet taste pretty different from each other in ways that have little to do with alcohol levels. Flavors in Napa Cabernet, even when aged, have more fruit flavors, while aged Bordeaux take on other flavors.

Do you drink both Napa Cabernet and Bordeaux and, if so, do you really find that they taste alike?

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked:
That is a great question Howard. Simple answer - in general they don’t taste alike - but in the last 20 years I think they have become less disimilar.

First - alcohol levels don’t really seem noticeable to me unless they are crazy high - say 16% which I think I have only seen in some Aussie Shiraz wines (must be some Cali wines that hit it with Syrah somewhere!) Shafer Hillside Select as an example is 15.5% - I can’t truthfully say I have found it to be off-putting because it is “hot.” But most Bordeaux are 14.5% or less which seems a bit higher than 20 years ago when they seemed more likely to be 13.5% or less - I need to get some data to confirm that impression but I am reasonably confident it is true. The other question is with a warmer climate to what extent are the Bordelaise less likely to perform Chaptalization - I am guessing that this practice is less common in say the last 20 years in Bordeaux - at least their haven’t been any riots over this practice in France in this century. Suffice to say, ceteris paribus I don’t think I am going to discern the difference in a Napa Cab and a Bordeaux from ABV alone.

I think that hotter weather and riper vintages in Bordeaux has changed the paradigm drastically since the 70’s. And I don’t think it has that it is exclusively the result of Michael Rolland or “Parkerization” (an adjective that undoubtedly will fade in the same way that people don’t say “Ruthian” any longer except for maybe Bob Costas.)

So yes, many Bordeaux wines I taste now are more likely to resemble Napa wines (which are always ripe except for a few notable years like 2011 or 2000). And a less ripe vintage like 2011 in Napa is more likely to resemble a Bordeaux. These are Broad strokes - but all things equal - as WK just mentioned - the clones are the same - so they have to taste different based on terroir and wine making. For me, the weather has the greatest influence and to the extent Bordeaux is hotter than it used to be the wines are more Napa like.

The other factor is wine making and foremost what percentages are in the wines - I am unaware of any Bordeaux that are 100% CS - that isn’t to say there aren’t some that are close - Lafite in 2019 is 94pct CS/5% Merlot/1pct PV. So again with broad strokes, unless you are comparing “meritage” wines from Napa I think you are going to get some nuance that is distinguishable solely based on the grapes and that many Napa Cabs may in fact not just be 80pct CS (to be designated as such) but 100 pct. In general, I find the blends from Napa are more Bordeaux like but I think that is the point for at least some wine makers - to emulate Bordeaux but in a very Napaesque manner.

Overall, Napa wines tend to be riper, more fruit forward, and less backward (tannic driven) although the best Napa Cabs have great tannin with phenolic ripeness - or as Parker would say “sweet tannins.” I think that is something that is more uniquely Napa and I don’t think I quite ever get with Bords even in ripe vintages . I don’t think I am going to confuse a 2002 Pichon Baron, a 2006 Pavie Macquin or a 2006 Montrose for a 2018 Schrader RBS - these are just few wines I have had recently so I will use them as examples. I think I could tell a 2020 Montrose from a 2020 Napa cab (and not because of smoke taint!). I had a 2020 Fleur Cardinale the other night - if thrown into a blind tasting of Cali Merlot blends - I am not sure I would be able to pick it out - stylistically if was very fruit forward and had a fairly ripe finish that was certainly Cali like.

Another point you allude to is aging. Laube (who may not be a universally favored critic but certainly venerable in some eyes and at least popular enough to be WS critic for several decades) had the general opinion that you just don’t age California wines. I kind of favor his thinking and with the exception of a few producers I find I drink most of my Cali wines within a decade of release - I do have a handful of older bottles - that single bottle of 2002 Dunn that may be ready?! There are some other producers that still make wines that are meant for the long haul - And I agree with you that aged Bordeaux - say 20 years and older develop into more interesting wines - I just can’t say that I have noticed that aged Napa gets a whole lot better - the fruit kind of fades and the tannins if ripe don’t need 20 years to settle down to put the wine in balance.

Final point - there are some flavors like green pepper (methoxypyrazines) that seem more unique to Bordeaux than Napa - I just recently learned that canopy management and sunlight on the grapes can actually affect this primary flavor. None the less, I just don’t pick up on this note in Napa Cabs maybe ever.

Probably need to do a couple of blind tastings of Bords vs Cali - the best way to be humbled.

Thanks for the long answer.

I admit that I am pretty selective with the California Cabernet I drink and a good many are not from Napa. But, most of the ones I own and drink are made for aging, not for drinking young. Some of wines I have a good bit of are Ridge Monte Bello, Chateau Montelena Estate, Togni, Mayacamas, Stony Hill and Mount Eden. I like my California Cabernet with age on them are rarely drink young Cabernet, except at a tasting or at someone else’s house.

But, then, I also don’t buy Rolland or other really modern-styled Bordeaux and don’t drink that much young Bordeaux either (again, except at a tasting or at someone else’s house), so maybe I have a skewed view of what the differences of the wines are like that are different from yours based on the wines we are drinking.

But, even with what I drink (and even for wines made years ago), I don’t think one could ever in the modern era (say 1982 Bordeaux and newer) be able to tell Bordeaux and California Cabernet apart reliably by weight of the wines or alcohol levels. It has been a while since I have participated in a blind tasting of California Cab vs. Bordeaux, but years ago when I did it more I thought the people at the tastings who tried to distinguish the wines by weight and alcohol levels were fooled more than people who tried to make the distinction based on flavor profiles. A friend of mine always complains that California Cabs do not age the same way as Bordeaux does and I answer him by saying isn’t that what you would expect with different terroirs. I like the fact that they don’t taste alike and don’t think that they should ever taste alike.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Esca is a fungal disease, no a viral infection, so its prevention is more to do with grafting and above all pruning decisions. Each pruning cut creates a cone of dead tissue inside the vine corresponding to the size of the wound; so if you want to avoid having lots of dead wood inside your vine, which can in turn open the door to esca, you need to place your pruning cuts in such a way as to leave the vine with unimpeded sap flows through to its extremities. Some pruning systems are more adapted to this than others. Unfortunately, pruning like this demands a bit of reflection and isn’t really compatible with contract labor that changes every year and watches the clock.

reply_to_user.name: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
On the pyrazines, there’s a fair amount of pyrazine-rich fruit in California, but that character doesn’t make it into the bottles of wine we are drinking. Pyrazines are almost universally hated here; many winemakers consider them a flaw. So fruit is left to hang until they go away, or they use flash detente to blow off the pyrazines, or they blend that lot away into a much larger one to hide the green pepper notes, or in the worst case they sell it as bulk. So you can find pyrazine character in some cheap or white label California Cabernet.

I love green pepper in Bordeaux blends and would like to see more of it, personally. But as Otto says, these days you don’t even get it in Bordeaux much of the time. Nowadays I have to go to the Loire or to New York Cab Franc or Chilean Carmenere to get my veggies.

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked:
I am convinced we all have different levels of sensitivity towards pyrazines, much like we do for coriander / cilantro. I might be hyper-sensitive because I still find them in many/most Bordeaux I try. I even perceive a bit of them in the Parkerized benchmark 2005 Pavie. So I am not sure I buy that modern Bordeaux has eliminated them.

I love a touch of pyrazines which is why I bought two cases of 2019 Ferriere, which tastes like top OG Bordeaux to me. But a little more, which others can find pleasant often overwhelm me, like in 2000 Sociando Maillet or most Loire cab francs, where they dominate the wine, making them truly unpleasant.

I think my sensitivity has decreased over the years, because even though I still skip the chunks of green pepper when eating, I appreciate the nuance they add to some dishes. In the past I’d straight up skip dishes that contained green pepper.

I haven’t consumed any top 2016+ Bordeaux, and would be fascinated / disappointed if they had truly eliminated pyrazines. To me they are a key element that differentiates Bordeaux.

For a Rollandized/Parkerized/Modern wine it seems pretty much like most Bordeaux to me - Still really good fruit - probably could use a bit more time to soften the finish a smidge but the tannins are distinctly Bordeaux at least for my palate.

I hated this wine on release and even 10 years out, glad it’s starting to seem more like a Bordeaux!

If you hate the ‘05, then don’t ever try the ‘04. I know that advice run contrary to the respective vintages’ reputations. I’ve always felt the ‘05 is an excellent Napa/Bdx crossover wine; the ‘04 is a liquified 2 x 4.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Pat_Martin:

Yeah, the 05 Lascombes on release was scary stuff (for my palate). A frankenwine. But knowing what Father Time will do to these brand new releases can be so hard – eg I would have never expected green to appear.

I think this may be a case where the palate sensitivity matters. I’ve had this 2005 and didn’t find it on the lean/mean/green side, rather more on that max Parker volume that was en vogue at the time.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

. . .

I personally am not betting on them, got rid of all of them. Glad others are enjoying them. The classics continue to shine. I reloaded on 2005 Montrose, gosh what a beauty.

Recognizing that I am on the other side of the aisle, I did buy some 2005 Bordeaux that you probably love to hate. A case each of Monbousquet and Haut Bergey. A bottle of Cos d’Estournel because I got a good deal, just to check it out.

I think it’s time to do an almost 20 years out retrospective - - blind so we can make fun of each other - - to see if the spoofulated, Parkerized wines with flavor or the elegant tutu wearing wines that taste like the dirt and poop on the corral floor, show better.

posts.cooked: If you liked them young, you’ll like them old. If not, not.Oak is always with us. Overripe fruit tastes like old overripe fruit after aging. Caterpillars don’t turn into hummingbirds. But you are right about one thing. They don’t fall down and die. And I have tasted both Monbousquet and Clos St. Jean with 15 years on them.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
I had an 1995 Monbousquet recently.

If my memory serve me well it was a Parker darling and impressed at tastings when young. It still has a pretty deep color. The oak tannins still dominate the wines profile. Lots of coffee in the nose (pretty dark barrel toasting I guess). I think too much extraction also.

I am not a fan. If additional cellaring will help is doubtful. Even when I consider that Bordeaux can be a chameleon changing its taste in a surprising manner sometimes. But the barrel toasting should not be so obvious after almost 30 years.
